background_cases: The development of sanitation and public health in Victorian Britain, which reduced infant mortality and raised life expectancy by several decades over the course of a century.
The spread of literacy in Prussia following the introduction of compulsory education in the eighteenth century, producing within a few generations one of the most literate populations in Europe.
The expansion of the railway network across the United States in the nineteenth century, which opened vast territories to settlement, commerce, and communication.
definition: A society has progressed if and only if (i) the material conditions of life for the majority of its members have measurably improved, (ii) the improvements are the result of deliberate human effort and planning, and (iii) the improvements are irreversible under normal circumstances.
conditions: material conditions for majority have measurably improved
improvements result from deliberate effort and planning
improvements are irreversible under normal circumstances
counterexample: The ecological transformation of the Australian continent following British colonisation produced, for the European settlers, a dramatic improvement in material conditions — vast tracts of land were brought under cultivation, towns were built, and a prosperous pastoral economy was established. Yet these improvements were not the result of deliberate planning directed at long-term progress; they were the largely uncoordinated consequences of individual settlers pursuing immediate economic advantage, often with devastating effects upon the indigenous population and the natural environment. The second condition fails, yet the transformation is routinely described as progress by those who measure it in material terms.
abductive_insight: The background cases — Victorian public health, Prussian education, American railways — were all instances of planned, state-directed improvement with broadly beneficial results. The definition abstracted deliberate planning as essential to progress, but the Australian case reveals that large-scale material improvement may emerge from dispersed, uncoordinated actions without any overarching plan, and that such improvement may be described as progress by some whilst constituting catastrophe for others.
passage:
Consider three cases commonly cited as instances of progress. The development of sanitation and public health in Victorian Britain, undertaken through deliberate legislation and municipal planning, which reduced infant mortality and extended life expectancy. The spread of literacy in Prussia following the introduction of compulsory schooling, a policy deliberately adopted by the state. The expansion of the American railway network, planned and financed by a combination of government land grants and private enterprise, which opened a continent to settlement and trade. From these cases one might extract a definition: a society has progressed if and only if the material conditions of life for the majority of its members have measurably improved, the improvements are the result of deliberate human effort and planning, and the improvements are irreversible under normal circumstances.

But this definition, drawn from cases of planned improvement in societies that broadly benefited, proves too restrictive when one considers the ecological and demographic transformation of Australia following British colonisation. For the European settlers, the material improvement was enormous. Within a few generations, a pastoral and agricultural economy of considerable prosperity had been established across vast territories. Towns, roads, and ports were built; living standards rose; the population grew rapidly. Yet these improvements were not the product of deliberate planning directed at progress. They were the largely uncoordinated consequences of thousands of individual settlers pursuing immediate economic advantage — claiming land, running sheep, clearing forests — without any overarching design. The transformation was as unplanned as it was consequential. Moreover, what constituted progress for the settlers constituted catastrophe for the indigenous population, whose lands were taken, whose societies were disrupted, and whose numbers were devastated. The background cases were all instances where planned effort yielded broad benefit. The definition mistook this conjunction of planning and benefit for an essential feature of progress, overlooking the possibility that material improvement may emerge without deliberate design, and that what counts as progress depends upon whose conditions one measures.

background_cases: The historian who explains the defeat of the Spanish Armada in 1588 by citing the English fleet's superior tactics, the disruption at Gravelines, and the storms that scattered the retreating ships.
The historian who explains the fall of Constantinople in 1453 by citing the Ottoman siege preparations, the weakness of the Byzantine garrison, and the breach in the walls.

The historian who explains the success of the Norman Conquest by citing William's military preparations, Harold's exhaustion after Stamford Bridge, and the tactical advantage of the cavalry charge at Hastings.
definition: An historical explanation is satisfactory if and only if (i) it identifies the principal agents whose decisions shaped the outcome, (ii) it shows how those decisions interacted with the circumstances to produce the event, and (iii) the explanation would be accepted as adequate by a competent contemporary observer who possessed all the relevant facts.
conditions: identifies principal agents whose decisions shaped outcome
shows how decisions interacted with circumstances
would be accepted by competent contemporary observer with all facts
counterexample: The explanation of the demographic collapse of indigenous American populations following European contact — which attributes the catastrophe primarily to the introduction of Old World diseases such as smallpox, measles, and influenza, against which indigenous populations had no immunity — identifies no principal agents whose decisions shaped the outcome. The disease transmission was largely inadvertent. No contemporary observer, however competent, could have understood the mechanism of epidemic disease in the terms available to modern epidemiology and immunology. The first and third conditions fail, yet the epidemiological explanation is regarded as the most important single factor in explaining the demographic transformation of the Americas.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all military events in which identifiable commanders made decisions that determined the outcome. The definition abstracted agency and contemporary intelligibility as essential, but the demographic collapse of the Americas was driven by impersonal biological processes that no agent intended and no contemporary could have understood, revealing that some of the most consequential historical explanations invoke causes that are neither agential nor accessible to the understanding of the period.
passage:
Consider three explanatory successes in military history. The historian explains the defeat of the Spanish Armada by citing English tactical superiority, the disruption at Gravelines, and the storms. The fall of Constantinople is explained by Ottoman siege preparations, Byzantine weakness, and the breach in the walls. The Norman Conquest is explained by William's preparations, Harold's exhaustion, and the cavalry at Hastings. In each case the historian identifies the agents whose decisions proved decisive and shows how those decisions, interacting with the circumstances, produced the outcome. From these cases one might define satisfactory historical explanation: an explanation is satisfactory if and only if it identifies the principal agents whose decisions shaped the outcome, shows how those decisions interacted with the circumstances, and would be accepted as adequate by a competent contemporary observer who possessed all the relevant facts.

But this definition, abstracted from cases of deliberate military action, proves inadequate when applied to one of the most consequential transformations in recorded history: the demographic collapse of indigenous American populations following European contact. The population of the Americas may have fallen by as much as ninety per cent in the century after 1492. The principal cause, as modern scholarship has established, was the introduction of Old World diseases — smallpox, measles, influenza, typhus — against which the indigenous peoples had no immunological defence. This explanation identifies no principal agents whose decisions shaped the outcome. The Spaniards did not deliberately deploy epidemic disease as a weapon; the transmission was largely inadvertent, a biological consequence of contact between populations with different disease histories. Nor would the explanation have been accepted by a competent contemporary observer: no one in the sixteenth century understood the germ theory of disease or the concept of immunological naivety. A contemporary observer, however intelligent, would have attributed the mortality to divine providence, miasma, or the natural constitution of the indigenous peoples. The background cases were all events shaped by identifiable human decisions. The definition mistook agency and contemporary intelligibility for essential features of explanation, when the most important cause of the demographic transformation of the Americas was an impersonal biological process that no one intended, no one controlled, and no one at the time could have understood.

background_cases: The division of English history at 1066, when the Norman Conquest replaced the Anglo-Saxon ruling class with a French-speaking aristocracy and transformed the legal and tenurial system.
The division of French history at 1789, when the Revolution destroyed the ancien régime and inaugurated a new political order.
The division of Russian history at 1917, when the Bolshevik Revolution overthrew the Tsarist autocracy and established a socialist state.
definition: A periodisation is well-founded if and only if (i) the boundary event is a singular, dateable occurrence, (ii) the character of political and social life differs fundamentally on either side of the boundary, and (iii) contemporaries recognised the event as inaugurating a new era.
conditions: boundary event is singular and dateable
political and social life differs fundamentally on either side
contemporaries recognised event as inaugurating new era
counterexample: The periodisation that divides European history into 'pre-industrial' and 'industrial' eras has no singular, dateable boundary event. The transition to industrial production occurred at different times in different countries — beginning in Britain around the 1760s, spreading to Belgium and France in the early nineteenth century, reaching Germany and the United States by mid-century, and arriving in Russia and Japan still later. No single event marks the boundary; the transformation was diffuse and protracted. Nor did contemporaries in, say, 1780 recognise themselves as living through the dawn of a new era. The third condition also fails. Yet the pre-industrial/industrial periodisation is among the most analytically powerful in historical scholarship.
abductive_insight: The background cases — 1066, 1789, 1917 — were all political ruptures: singular events that contemporaries experienced as transformative. The definition abstracted singularity, fundamental difference, and contemporary recognition as essential, but the industrial periodisation reveals that the most analytically important divisions of the past may correspond to diffuse, multi-generational transformations that no single event inaugurated and no contemporary fully recognised.
passage:
Consider three well-established periodisations. The division of English history at 1066, when the Norman Conquest overthrew the Anglo-Saxon order. The division of French history at 1789, when the Revolution destroyed the ancien régime. The division of Russian history at 1917, when the Bolsheviks seized power. In each case the boundary is a singular, dateable event; the character of life changed fundamentally on either side; and contemporaries were acutely aware that something momentous had occurred. From these cases one might define a well-founded periodisation: it is well-founded if and only if the boundary event is singular and dateable, political and social life differs fundamentally on either side, and contemporaries recognised the event as inaugurating a new era.

But this definition, drawn from dramatic political ruptures, proves too narrow when one considers the periodisation that divides European history into pre-industrial and industrial eras. This division is among the most analytically powerful in modern historical scholarship. It captures a transformation in the material conditions of human life more profound than any political revolution: the shift from an economy based on human and animal muscle, wind, and water to one powered by fossil fuels and machinery. Yet the boundary has no singular, dateable event. The transition to industrial production began in Britain around the 1760s with developments in textile manufacturing and iron production, but it unfolded over decades and spread unevenly across Europe and beyond. Belgium and France industrialised in the early nineteenth century; Germany and the United States followed by mid-century; Russia and Japan came later still. There is no single moment at which the pre-industrial world ended and the industrial world began. Nor did contemporaries recognise the transformation as it occurred. A cotton spinner in Lancashire in 1780 did not suppose himself to be living at the dawn of a new era; he was simply tending his machine. The background cases were all political revolutions whose singularity and drama made them visible to contemporaries. The definition mistook this visibility for an essential property of periodisation, overlooking the slow, diffuse, and impersonal transformations that may be the most consequential divisions in human history.

background_cases: Ancient Egypt, whose civilisational continuity is traced through thousands of years of dynastic succession, with each pharaoh building upon the cultural and architectural legacy of his predecessors.
The Chinese civilisation, whose continuity from the Shang dynasty to the present is sustained by the persistence of the written language, Confucian ethics, and a tradition of centralised governance.

The civilisation of ancient Rome, whose continuity from the founding of the city to the fall of the Western Empire is marked by the persistence of Latin, Roman law, and the institutions of the Roman state.
definition: A civilisation maintains its identity over time if and only if (i) there is institutional continuity between its earlier and later phases, (ii) the language of public life remains the same or develops in an unbroken line, and (iii) the inhabitants regard themselves as belonging to the same civilisation as their ancestors.
conditions: institutional continuity between earlier and later phases
language of public life remains same or develops continuously
inhabitants regard themselves as belonging to same civilisation as ancestors
counterexample: The civilisation of ancient Greece is treated as a continuous entity from the Mycenaean age through the classical period, yet the collapse of Mycenaean palatial civilisation around 1200 BCE produced a period of several centuries — the so-called Dark Ages — during which the institutions of Mycenaean society vanished, literacy was lost (Linear B ceased to be used, and the Greek alphabet was not developed until centuries later), and there is no evidence that the inhabitants of eighth-century Greece regarded themselves as heirs to the Mycenaean world. All three conditions fail for the transition from Mycenaean to Archaic Greece, yet scholars treat Greek civilisation as a continuous entity.
abductive_insight: The background cases — Egypt, China, Rome — were all civilisations characterised by unusual institutional and linguistic continuity. The definition abstracted these features as essential to civilisational identity, but the Greek case reveals that a civilisation may survive a catastrophic interruption in its institutions, its literacy, and its self-understanding, maintaining its identity through deeper continuities of settlement, material culture, and the eventual recovery of its mythological and heroic traditions.
passage:
Consider three civilisations celebrated for their continuity. Ancient Egypt, whose dynastic succession sustained a recognisable cultural identity over millennia. China, whose written language, Confucian ethics, and tradition of centralised governance have persisted from antiquity to the present. Rome, whose language, law, and political institutions provided a continuous thread from the city's founding to the fall of the Western Empire. From these cases one might extract a definition: a civilisation maintains its identity over time if and only if there is institutional continuity between its earlier and later phases, the language of public life remains the same or develops in an unbroken line, and the inhabitants regard themselves as belonging to the same civilisation as their ancestors.

But this definition, drawn from civilisations of extraordinary continuity, encounters a severe difficulty when applied to ancient Greece. Historians and philosophers of history routinely speak of Greek civilisation as a continuous entity, extending from the Mycenaean palaces of the second millennium BCE through the Archaic, Classical, and Hellenistic periods. Yet the transition from the Mycenaean to the Archaic age involved a rupture of the most thoroughgoing kind. Around 1200 BCE, Mycenaean palatial civilisation collapsed. The great palaces at Mycenae, Tiryns, and Pylos were destroyed or abandoned. The elaborate bureaucratic institutions that had administered the Mycenaean kingdoms vanished. Literacy was lost: the Linear B script, used for palace records, ceased to be written, and Greece entered a period of several centuries — the so-called Dark Ages — during which no written records were produced. When literacy re-emerged, it took the form of an entirely new script, the Greek alphabet, borrowed from the Phoenicians. There is no evidence that the inhabitants of eighth-century Greece regarded themselves as heirs to the Mycenaean world; the Mycenaean palaces were ruins whose original purpose was forgotten. All three conditions fail for this transition. The background cases were civilisations whose continuity was sustained by persistent institutions, languages, and self-identification. The definition mistook these features for universal requirements of civilisational identity, when Greek civilisation survived a catastrophic break in its institutional, linguistic, and psychological continuity — maintained, perhaps, by deeper and less visible threads: the continuity of settlement, the persistence of material culture, and the eventual recovery, through oral tradition, of the heroic memory of the Mycenaean age.

background_cases: The decline of the Western Roman Empire, marked by the loss of provinces to barbarian peoples, the weakening of the central government, and the eventual deposition of the last emperor in 476.
The decline of Mughal India, visible in the progressive loss of provincial control, the rise of regional successor states, and the inability of later emperors to exercise effective sovereignty.
The decline of the Spanish Empire in the seventeenth century, characterised by military defeats, financial exhaustion, and the loss of European and colonial territories.
definition: A civilisation is in decline if and only if (i) it loses control over territories it formerly governed, (ii) it is defeated in military conflicts with rising rival powers, and (iii) its central government becomes progressively weaker and less capable of enforcing its authority.
conditions: loses control over formerly governed territories
defeated in military conflicts with rising rivals
central government becomes progressively weaker
counterexample: The Byzantine Empire in the age of Justinian — the sixth century — had recently reconquered North Africa, Italy, and parts of Spain, was militarily formidable, and possessed a strong central government in Constantinople. None of the three conditions of decline obtained. Yet the costs of Justinian's reconquests overstrained the empire's resources, the plague of 541 devastated its population, and within a century the reconquered territories were lost again. The civilisation was, in a deeper sense, in decline even during its most spectacular phase of military expansion, because the expansion itself was unsustainable and masked the erosion of the demographic and fiscal foundations upon which the empire depended.
abductive_insight: The background cases — Rome, Mughal India, Spain — were all instances in which decline was visible in territorial loss, military defeat, and governmental weakness. The definition abstracted these external symptoms as constitutive of decline, but the Byzantine case under Justinian reveals that a civilisation may be in decline even during a period of military triumph and territorial expansion, if the expansion itself is unsustainable and conceals the depletion of the deeper resources — demographic, fiscal, ecological — upon which long-term vitality depends.
passage:
Consider three paradigmatic cases of civilisational decline. The Western Roman Empire, which lost its provinces one by one to barbarian peoples, suffered repeated military reverses, and saw its central government dwindle into impotence before the last emperor was deposed in 476. Mughal India, whose later emperors watched helplessly as provincial governors became independent rulers and the empire fragmented. The Spanish Empire of the seventeenth century, which endured military defeats, financial ruin, and the steady loss of its European and colonial possessions. From these cases one might define decline: a civilisation is in decline if and only if it loses control over territories it formerly governed, it is defeated in military conflicts with rising rival powers, and its central government becomes progressively weaker.

But this definition, drawn from cases in which decline was externally visible, proves misleading when applied to the Byzantine Empire under Justinian in the sixth century. Justinian's reign was, on its face, a period of spectacular revival. His generals reconquered North Africa from the Vandals, recovered Italy from the Ostrogoths, and seized parts of southern Spain. The Byzantine army was formidable; the central government in Constantinople was strong, efficient, and ambitious. None of the three conditions of decline obtained. Yet the costs of these reconquests were ruinous. The treasury was drained; the army was overstretched; the provinces were exhausted by taxation. And in 541, the first great plague pandemic struck the empire, killing perhaps a quarter of its population and inflicting a demographic wound from which Byzantium never fully recovered. Within a century of Justinian's death, the reconquered territories were lost — North Africa and Spain to the Arabs, much of Italy to the Lombards. The civilisation had been in decline even during its most triumphant phase, because the triumph was purchased at a cost the empire could not sustain. The background cases were all instances in which decline manifested as visible territorial and military contraction. The definition mistook these symptoms for the essence of decline, overlooking the possibility that a civilisation may be declining most dangerously at the very moment when it appears most powerful, if its expansion conceals the depletion of the foundations upon which its power rests.

background_cases: A narrative of the English Civil War that traces the conflict from the king's attempt to arrest the five members in January 1642 through the battles of Edgehill, Marston Moor, and Naseby to the king's execution in 1649, presenting the outcome as the intelligible consequence of the preceding events.

A narrative of the unification of Italy that begins with the Risorgimento's intellectual origins and proceeds through the campaigns of Garibaldi and Cavour to the proclamation of the Kingdom of Italy in 1861.
A narrative of the American Revolution that traces the path from the Stamp Act crisis through the Continental Congresses to the Declaration of Independence and the Treaty of Paris.
definition: An historical narrative is adequate if and only if (i) it renders the outcome intelligible by showing how earlier events made later events probable, (ii) it is organised around the actions of identifiable historical agents, and (iii) it culminates in a resolution that brings the sequence of events to a natural close.
conditions: renders outcome intelligible by showing how earlier events made later probable
organised around actions of identifiable agents
culminates in resolution bringing sequence to natural close
counterexample: A narrative of the transformation of European agriculture in the eighteenth century — the enclosure of common fields, the introduction of crop rotation, the selective breeding of livestock — cannot be organised around the actions of identifiable agents, for the transformation was the aggregate result of thousands of individual decisions by landlords, tenants, and labourers across the continent. Nor does it culminate in a resolution; the transformation had no end-point, shading gradually into the industrial agriculture of the nineteenth century. The second and third conditions fail, yet agricultural historians produce narratives of this transformation that are regarded as both coherent and illuminating.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all political and military narratives organised around great men and decisive events. The definition abstracted agential organisation and narrative closure as essential, but the narrative of agricultural transformation reveals that adequate historical narratives may be organised around impersonal processes and structural changes that have no identifiable protagonists and no natural terminus.
passage:
Consider three successful historical narratives. A narrative of the English Civil War, tracing the conflict from the king's provocations through the great battles to the execution of Charles I — a sequence organised around identifiable agents whose decisions drove events towards a definite resolution. A narrative of Italian unification, structured around Garibaldi, Cavour, and Mazzini, culminating in the proclamation of the kingdom. A narrative of the American Revolution, following the colonial leaders from protest to independence. In each case the narrative renders the outcome intelligible, is organised around the actions of identifiable persons, and reaches a natural close. From these cases one might define adequate historical narrative: it is adequate if and only if it renders the outcome intelligible by showing how earlier events made later events probable, it is organised around the actions of identifiable agents, and it culminates in a resolution that brings the sequence to a natural close.

But this definition, abstracted from political and military narratives centred upon great men and decisive moments, proves too restrictive when one turns to the history of structural transformation. Consider the narrative of the agricultural revolution in eighteenth-century Europe. Over the course of several generations, the open-field system that had dominated European farming for centuries was replaced by enclosed, individually managed holdings. New techniques of crop rotation were adopted; root crops were introduced as winter fodder; livestock were selectively bred for size and yield. The cumulative effect was a transformation in agricultural productivity that made possible the feeding of a rapidly growing population and provided the surplus labour that would fuel industrialisation. Agricultural historians narrate this transformation with skill and insight. Yet their narratives cannot be organised around the actions of identifiable agents. No single person drove the enclosure movement; it was the aggregate consequence of thousands of decisions by landlords, parliamentary committees, tenants, and labourers, each acting in his own interest and none conceiving of himself as part of a larger transformation. Nor does the narrative reach a natural resolution: the agricultural revolution shaded gradually into the mechanised farming of the nineteenth century, with no decisive moment at which one could say the transformation was complete. The background cases were all narratives of political drama with identifiable protagonists and satisfying conclusions. The definition mistook these features for requirements of all adequate narrative, overlooking the narratives of impersonal, structural change that are among the most important achievements of modern historiography.

background_cases: The English Revolution of the 1640s, in which Parliament took up arms against the Crown, defeated the royalist forces in battle, and established — however briefly — a republican Commonwealth.
The American Revolution of 1775-1783, in which the colonists organised militias, fought a war of independence against Britain, and established a new republic.
The Russian Revolution of 1917, in which the Bolsheviks seized power through an armed insurrection, overthrew the Provisional Government, and imposed a new social and political order by force.
definition: An historical event constitutes a revolution if and only if (i) it is initiated by a self-conscious revolutionary movement that explicitly seeks to overturn the existing order, (ii) the movement employs armed force to seize control of the state, and (iii) the resulting transformation is imposed from above by the victorious revolutionary leadership.
conditions: initiated by self-conscious revolutionary movement seeking to overturn existing order
movement employs armed force to seize state control
transformation imposed from above by victorious leadership
counterexample: The Printing Revolution of the fifteenth and sixteenth centuries transformed the intellectual, religious, and political life of Europe as profoundly as any armed upheaval. The mass production of books undermined the Church's monopoly on learning, made the Reformation possible, and created the conditions for the Scientific Revolution. Yet no self-conscious movement sought to bring about this transformation. Gutenberg and his successors were craftsmen and businessmen, not revolutionaries. No armed force was employed; no state was seized. The transformation was not imposed from above but emerged from below, through the dispersed commercial decisions of printers, booksellers, and readers across the continent. All three conditions fail, yet the Printing Revolution reshaped European civilisation.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the English, American, and Russian revolutions — were all political upheavals driven by organised movements that consciously sought to seize state power by force. The definition abstracted revolutionary consciousness, armed seizure, and top-down imposition as essential, but the Printing Revolution reveals that the most far-reaching revolutions may be technological and cultural rather than political, emerging not from any conscious revolutionary project but from the cumulative, uncoordinated effects of a transformative innovation.
passage:
Consider three revolutions that have shaped the modern world. The English Revolution, in which Parliament took up arms against the Crown and, after years of civil war, established a republic. The American Revolution, in which the colonists organised armed resistance to British rule and founded a new nation. The Russian Revolution, in which the Bolsheviks seized power through insurrection and imposed a new order by force. In each case, a self-conscious revolutionary movement explicitly sought to overturn the existing political regime, employed armed force to seize control of the state, and imposed its vision of a transformed society from above. From these cases one might define revolution: an historical event constitutes a revolution if and only if it is initiated by a self-conscious movement that seeks to overturn the existing order, the movement employs armed force to seize state control, and the resulting transformation is imposed from above by the victorious leadership.

But this definition, drawn from political revolutions driven by conscious agents wielding military power, proves altogether too narrow when one considers the Printing Revolution. The introduction of movable type by Gutenberg in the mid-fifteenth century and the subsequent spread of printing across Europe transformed the intellectual, religious, and political life of the continent as profoundly as any armed upheaval. Within a century, the production of books increased from a trickle of hand-copied manuscripts to a flood of printed volumes. The Church's near-monopoly on the dissemination of learning was broken. The Reformation became possible in part because Luther's theses and tracts could be printed and distributed across Germany in a matter of weeks. The Scientific Revolution of the seventeenth century depended upon the ability of scholars to publish their findings and read one another's work. Yet no self-conscious revolutionary movement sought to bring about this transformation. Gutenberg was a craftsman seeking to profit from a technical innovation, not a revolutionary seeking to overturn an order. No armed force was employed. No state was seized. The transformation was not imposed from above but emerged from below, through the dispersed and uncoordinated decisions of thousands of printers, booksellers, scholars, and readers. The background cases were all political revolutions driven by revolutionary consciousness and military force. The definition mistook these features for the essence of revolution, overlooking the technological and cultural revolutions that may reshape civilisation more profoundly than any seizure of power, precisely because they transform the conditions under which all political and intellectual life is conducted.

background_cases: Ancient Egypt, with its monumental architecture, its hieroglyphic script, its complex system of administration, and its elaborate religious rituals, is recognised as one of the great civilisations of antiquity.
The Roman Empire, with its roads, aqueducts, legal codes, and extensive system of provincial government, is recognised as a civilisation of immense achievement and influence.

The Chinese Empire under the Tang dynasty, with its poetry, its painting, its civil service examinations, and its sophisticated bureaucratic administration, is recognised as one of the high points of human civilisation.
definition: A society S constitutes a civilisation if and only if (i) S possesses monumental architecture — temples, palaces, or public buildings of imposing scale, (ii) S is governed by a centralised state with a professional bureaucracy, and (iii) S possesses a system of writing used for administration and record-keeping.
conditions: S possesses monumental architecture
S is governed by a centralised state with a professional bureaucracy
S possesses a system of writing used for administration and record-keeping
counterexample: Consider the Inca Empire. The Inca created a society of extraordinary complexity and achievement, extending over thousands of miles of the Andes and governing a population of millions. They constructed monumental architecture — Machu Picchu, Sacsayhuaman, the great temple of the sun in Cuzco — and they were governed by a centralised state with an elaborate administrative hierarchy. Yet they possessed no system of writing in the conventional sense. They recorded information using quipus — knotted strings — which served administrative and perhaps narrative purposes, but which are not writing in the sense of a script that encodes spoken language. The third condition fails. Yet the Inca Empire is recognised by historians as a civilisation of remarkable sophistication.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all literate civilisations — Egypt with its hieroglyphs, Rome with its Latin script, Tang China with its logographic writing — in which writing was central to administration and cultural life. The definition abstracted monumental architecture, centralised bureaucracy, and writing as jointly essential to civilisation. But the cases were drawn from the Old World, where writing developed independently in several centres, and the definition mistook the features of literate civilisations for the features of civilisation as such. The Inca Empire reveals that a society may achieve the highest levels of political, architectural, and administrative complexity without possessing writing in the conventional sense. The definition confused one particular instrument of civilisational organisation — the written script — with a necessary condition of civilisation itself.
passage:
Consider three societies that are recognised as great civilisations. Ancient Egypt, with its pyramids, its temples at Karnak and Luxor, its hieroglyphic script, and its elaborate system of administration extending from the Pharaoh through the vizier to the provincial governors. The Roman Empire, with its roads spanning three continents, its aqueducts supplying great cities, its legal codes governing millions, and its professional bureaucracy administering provinces from Britain to Mesopotamia. The Chinese Empire under the Tang dynasty, with its poetry and painting, its civil service recruited by competitive examination, and its bureaucratic administration of an immense and diverse territory. From these cases one might extract a definition: a society constitutes a civilisation if and only if it possesses monumental architecture, is governed by a centralised state with a professional bureaucracy, and possesses a system of writing used for administration and record-keeping.

But one must consider the Inca Empire. The Inca governed a territory stretching more than two thousand miles along the spine of the Andes, from present-day Colombia to central Chile, and administered a population numbering in the millions. They constructed monumental architecture of astonishing quality — the citadel of Machu Picchu, the fortress of Sacsayhuaman with its walls of precisely fitted stones weighing many tonnes, the great temple of the Sun in Cuzco with its walls lined with sheets of gold. They were governed by a centralised state of considerable sophistication, with an elaborate hierarchy of officials, a system of roads connecting the provinces to the capital, relay runners carrying messages across vast distances, and storehouses provisioned against famine. By any reasonable reckoning, the Inca Empire was a civilisation. Yet the Inca possessed no system of writing in the conventional sense. They did not encode their language in a script of symbols inscribed upon a surface. They recorded information by means of quipus — assemblages of knotted and coloured strings — which served purposes of accounting, census-taking, and perhaps narrative, but which are not writing as the term is ordinarily understood. The third condition of the definition fails. The background cases were all literate civilisations of the Old World, and the definition mistook the presence of conventional writing — a feature common to those particular civilisations — for a necessary condition of civilisation as such, overlooking the possibility that a society might achieve the highest levels of complexity and achievement by means other than the written word.

background_cases: The progress of medical knowledge from Hippocrates to Galen to Vesalius to Harvey, in which each generation corrected the errors of its predecessors and advanced the understanding of the human body.
The progress of astronomical knowledge from Ptolemy to Copernicus to Kepler to Newton, in which successive theories provided more accurate accounts of the motions of the heavenly bodies.

The progress of chemical knowledge from the alchemists through Boyle and Lavoisier to Dalton, in which vague and mystical speculation was gradually replaced by precise, experimentally grounded theory.
definition: A domain of human activity D exhibits genuine progress if and only if (i) later achievements in D are superior to earlier ones by a single, objective standard of measurement, (ii) the improvement is cumulative — each generation builds upon the achievements of the last without losing what was gained, and (iii) the practitioners of D are conscious of the improvement and regard their own work as superior to that of their predecessors.
conditions: later achievements are superior to earlier ones by a single, objective standard
the improvement is cumulative — each generation builds upon the last
the practitioners are conscious of the improvement and regard their work as superior
counterexample: Consider the progress of the novel from the eighteenth century to the twentieth. Later novels are not superior to earlier ones by any single, objective standard of measurement. One cannot say that Ulysses is objectively superior to Tom Jones, or that Proust surpasses Fielding, by any metric analogous to the accuracy of astronomical prediction. The improvement, if it is improvement, is not straightforwardly cumulative; the achievements of the eighteenth-century novel were not preserved and built upon in the manner of scientific results, and later novelists often rejected the conventions of their predecessors rather than extending them. And the practitioners are not uniformly conscious of improvement; many twentieth-century novelists have expressed admiration for their eighteenth-century forebears without regarding their own work as superior. Yet few would deny that the art of the novel has progressed in some meaningful sense — that the range of techniques, perspectives, and expressive possibilities available to the novelist has expanded enormously.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all drawn from the natural sciences — medicine, astronomy, chemistry — where progress is measured by a single standard (accuracy, explanatory power), where improvement is cumulative, and where practitioners are conscious of surpassing their predecessors. The definition abstracted these features as essential to genuine progress. But the cases were drawn from a domain in which progress takes a particularly clear and measurable form, and the definition mistook the features of scientific progress for the features of progress as such. The novel reveals that progress may occur in domains where there is no single objective standard of measurement, where improvement is not straightforwardly cumulative, and where practitioners may not regard their own work as superior to that of the past. The definition confused the clarity of scientific advance with the more elusive and multidimensional character of progress in the arts and humanities.
passage:
Consider three domains in which progress is widely acknowledged. The progress of medical knowledge from Hippocrates to Harvey, in which each generation corrected the errors of its predecessors and advanced the understanding of the human body. The progress of astronomy from Ptolemy to Newton, in which successive theories provided more accurate predictions of celestial motions. The progress of chemistry from the alchemists to Dalton, in which vague speculation gave way to precise, experimentally grounded theory. From these cases one might extract a definition: a domain of human activity exhibits genuine progress if and only if later achievements are superior to earlier ones by a single, objective standard, the improvement is cumulative, and the practitioners are conscious of the improvement.

But one must consider the art of the novel. The novel has developed enormously since its emergence in the eighteenth century. The range of narrative techniques available to the novelist has expanded — stream of consciousness, unreliable narration, fragmented chronology, the dissolution of the boundary between narrator and character. The subjects treated have broadened; the interior life, the unconscious, the experience of marginalised peoples have all been brought within the compass of the form. The expressive possibilities of prose fiction are incomparably richer today than they were in the age of Richardson and Fielding. Yet the progress of the novel does not satisfy the conditions of the definition. Later novels are not superior to earlier ones by any single, objective standard of measurement. There is no metric of novelistic achievement analogous to the accuracy of astronomical prediction; one cannot demonstrate that Joyce is objectively superior to Fielding in the way that one can demonstrate that Newton is more accurate than Ptolemy. The improvement is not straightforwardly cumulative. The achievements of the eighteenth-century novel — its command of social observation, its moral directness, its robust narrative energy — were not simply preserved and built upon by later generations; many were abandoned or rejected. And the practitioners are not uniformly conscious of surpassing their predecessors; many of the greatest modern novelists have expressed profound admiration for earlier writers without claiming superiority. The definition, drawn from the sciences, mistook the clear, measurable, cumulative character of scientific progress for the essential features of progress as such, and thereby excluded progress in domains where advancement is real but resists quantification.

background_cases: The division of European history at the fall of the Western Roman Empire in 476 marks a clear boundary: one political order ends and another begins, and the date can be specified with precision.

The division of English history at the Norman Conquest of 1066 marks a definite boundary: one dynasty is replaced by another, the language and law of the realm are transformed, and the date is fixed by a single battle.
The division of French history at the Revolution of 1789 marks a boundary that can be located precisely: the storming of the Bastille inaugurates a new political and social order.
definition: A division of history into periods is valid if and only if (i) the division corresponds to a decisive political event — a conquest, a revolution, a change of dynasty — that marks the transition from one period to the next, (ii) the event in question produces a rapid and comprehensive transformation of the society, and (iii) the characteristics of the new period are fundamentally different from those of the old — that is, the two periods share no important features.
conditions: the division corresponds to a decisive political event
the event produces rapid and comprehensive transformation
the characteristics of the new period are fundamentally different from the old
counterexample: Consider the periodisation of economic history into the pre-industrial and the industrial ages. This division is universally employed by economic historians and is of the first importance for understanding the modern world. But the transition from the pre-industrial to the industrial age does not correspond to any single decisive political event. It was not a conquest, a revolution, or a change of dynasty, but a gradual transformation of the means of production — the introduction of machinery, the harnessing of steam power, the development of the factory system — that proceeded unevenly across regions and decades. The transformation was not rapid; it extended over a century or more, beginning in the textile mills of Lancashire in the 1760s and spreading only gradually to other industries and other countries. And the characteristics of the new period were not fundamentally different from those of the old in every respect; many features of pre-industrial life — the institution of private property, the structure of the family, the basic forms of religious observance — persisted well into the industrial age. All three conditions fail, yet the periodisation is valid and indispensable.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the fall of Rome, the Norman Conquest, the French Revolution — were all political events of dramatic character that produced sudden and visible transformations. The definition abstracted the political, the sudden, and the comprehensive as essential features of valid periodisation. But the background cases were drawn from the domain of political and military history, where periods are often defined by dramatic ruptures, and the definition mistook the features of that domain for the features of periodisation in general. The transition from pre-industrial to industrial society reveals that the most important divisions in history may be economic rather than political, gradual rather than sudden, and partial rather than comprehensive. The definition confused the mode of periodisation appropriate to political history with the general conditions of valid periodisation.
passage:
Consider three divisions of history that are widely accepted as valid. The fall of the Western Roman Empire in 476 marks a boundary between the ancient and the medieval worlds; the deposition of the last emperor is a decisive political event, the transformation of the Western provinces is rapid and far-reaching, and the characteristics of the medieval world — feudalism, the dominance of the Church, the fragmentation of political authority — are fundamentally different from those of the Roman Empire. The Norman Conquest of 1066 marks a boundary in English history; a single battle replaces one dynasty with another, transforms the language and the law, and inaugurates a new order. The French Revolution of 1789 marks a boundary in French and European history; the storming of the Bastille is a decisive event, the transformation of the political and social order is rapid, and the characteristics of post-Revolutionary France are profoundly different from those of the ancien regime. From these cases one might extract a definition: a division of history into periods is valid if and only if it corresponds to a decisive political event, the event produces rapid and comprehensive transformation, and the characteristics of the new period are fundamentally different from those of the old.

But one must consider the periodisation of economic history into the pre-industrial and the industrial ages. This division is of the first importance for economic historians and for anyone who seeks to understand the conditions of modern life. It separates a world in which production was carried on by human and animal muscle, by wind and water, and by the hand-tools of the craftsman, from a world in which production is carried on by machinery driven by inanimate sources of power, in factories employing hundreds or thousands of workers. The division is universally employed and universally regarded as valid. Yet it does not correspond to any single decisive political event. No conquest inaugurated the industrial age; no revolution proclaimed it; no change of dynasty marked its beginning. The transformation was economic and technological — the introduction of the spinning jenny, the development of the steam engine, the construction of the factory — and it proceeded with agonising slowness. The first cotton mills appeared in Lancashire in the 1760s, but the transformation of the iron industry was not complete until the 1830s, the railway network was not established until the 1850s, and large parts of Europe remained pre-industrial well into the twentieth century. The transformation was not rapid but extended over a century or more, and it was not comprehensive but affected different industries, different regions, and different classes at very different rates. Nor were the characteristics of the new period fundamentally different from those of the old in every respect. The institution of private property persisted; the family retained its basic structure; the forms of religious observance continued largely unchanged. The background cases were political events of dramatic character, and the definition took drama, suddenness, and comprehensiveness as essential to valid periodisation. The industrial revolution reveals that the most consequential divisions in history may be economic, gradual, and uneven, and that periodisation need not rest upon a single spectacular event.

background_cases: The transition from hand-copying manuscripts to movable-type printing in fifteenth-century Europe is widely regarded as progressive, because it vastly increased the availability of written material and thereby broadened access to learning.
The abolition of serfdom in nineteenth-century Russia is regarded as progressive, because it removed a legal framework that had subjected millions of people to the arbitrary authority of landowners.
The development of anaesthesia in the mid-nineteenth century is regarded as progressive, because it eliminated the agony that had previously accompanied surgical procedures.

The extension of the franchise to women in the early twentieth century is regarded as progressive, because it removed a legal barrier that had excluded half the adult population from political participation.
definition: A change C in a society constitutes progress if and only if (i) C increases the material or technical capacities available to the members of the society, (ii) C removes or reduces a form of suffering, coercion, or exclusion, and (iii) C is irreversible in the sense that the conditions that made the earlier state of affairs stable have been permanently altered.
conditions: C increases material or technical capacities
C removes or reduces suffering, coercion, or exclusion
C is irreversible — the conditions sustaining the earlier state have been permanently altered
counterexample: Consider the introduction of leaded petrol in the early twentieth century. It increased material and technical capacities — engines ran more smoothly, more efficiently, and with greater power. It could be said to have reduced certain forms of hardship, inasmuch as improved transportation diminished the burdens of distance. And the change appeared irreversible — the infrastructure of petroleum refining and automotive engineering was permanently altered. All three conditions were satisfied. Yet the introduction of leaded petrol poisoned the air, the soil, and the blood of millions of people over several decades, causing widespread neurological damage, particularly among children in urban areas. A change that satisfies the three conditions may nonetheless constitute regress when its harmful consequences, unforeseen at the time, outweigh its benefits.
abductive_insight: The background cases involved changes whose benefits were broadly distributed, whose costs were minimal, and whose harmful consequences, if any, were negligible compared with the gains. The definition captures the positive dimensions of progress but drops any condition concerning the harmful side-effects of the change — the requirement that the gains not be outweighed by new forms of suffering or damage.
passage:
When one considers the changes that are most unambiguously regarded as progressive — the invention of printing, the abolition of serfdom, the development of anaesthesia, the extension of the franchise — a common pattern emerges. Each increased the capacities available to members of the society, each removed or diminished a form of suffering or exclusion, and each was, in a practical sense, irreversible: the conditions that had sustained the earlier arrangement had been so thoroughly altered that return to the previous state was unthinkable. One might distil from these cases the following definition: a change constitutes progress if and only if it increases material or technical capacities, removes or reduces suffering, coercion, or exclusion, and is irreversible.

Yet this definition, though it captures the positive features of the paradigm cases, is blind to a possibility that the history of technology illustrates with uncomfortable frequency. Consider the introduction of tetraethyl lead as a petrol additive in the early twentieth century. The change increased technical capacities: engines ran more smoothly and more efficiently. It could be said to have reduced certain hardships of transportation. And the change was irreversible in the specified sense — the infrastructure of petroleum refining and automotive manufacture was permanently reconfigured. All three conditions appear to be met. Yet the introduction of leaded petrol resulted in the widespread contamination of air, soil, and water, causing neurological damage to millions of people, particularly children in densely populated areas. The damage accumulated silently for decades before it was recognised. The background cases of progress were cases in which the benefits were broadly distributed and the costs minimal; the definition has abstracted the beneficial dimensions but has neglected to include any condition requiring that the change not introduce new forms of harm that outweigh the gains. What is missing is a condition concerning the net effect of the change — a requirement that the benefits not be purchased at a cost greater than the benefits themselves.

background_cases: Thucydides' explanation of the Peloponnesian War identifies not merely the immediate provocations — the disputes over Corcyra and Potidaea — but the underlying cause: the growth of Athenian power and the fear this inspired in Sparta.

Tocqueville's explanation of the French Revolution identifies not merely the immediate triggers — fiscal crisis, the calling of the Estates-General — but the long-term structural causes: the centralisation of royal power, the decay of the aristocracy, and the rise of egalitarian sentiment.

Braudel's explanation of the decline of Mediterranean trade identifies not merely individual events but the slow-moving structural changes in geography, climate, and economic organisation that shaped the possibilities of commerce over centuries.
definition: An account A constitutes an adequate historical explanation of an event E if and only if (i) A identifies the immediate causes or triggers of E, (ii) A places E within a broader temporal context by identifying long-term structural conditions that made E possible, and (iii) A is supported by documentary evidence.
conditions: A identifies the immediate causes or triggers of E
A places E within a broader temporal context of structural conditions
A is supported by documentary evidence
counterexample: Consider an explanation of the Irish Famine of the 1840s that identifies the immediate cause — the potato blight caused by Phytophthora infestans — and places the famine within the broader structural context of Irish dependence on a single crop, colonial land tenure, and chronic poverty. Documentary evidence for both the blight and the structural conditions is abundant. All three conditions are met. Yet the explanation omits the role of British government policy — the continued export of grain from Ireland during the famine, the inadequacy of relief measures, the ideological commitment to laissez-faire economics — which many historians regard as indispensable to any adequate explanation. The explanation identifies structural conditions and immediate causes but fails to account for the human agency that shaped the course of events.
abductive_insight: The background cases — Thucydides, Tocqueville, Braudel — all involved explanations that attended not only to structural conditions and immediate triggers but to the decisions, perceptions, and agency of the actors involved. The definition captures the structural and evidential dimensions but drops the requirement that an adequate explanation account for the role of human agency in shaping the course of events.
passage:
The great historians — Thucydides, Tocqueville, Braudel — have in common a capacity to move between the immediate and the structural, explaining events by situating them within larger patterns of cause and condition. Thucydides identifies not merely the proximate provocations of the Peloponnesian War but the underlying dynamic of Athenian expansion and Spartan fear. Tocqueville traces the French Revolution not merely to the fiscal crisis of 1789 but to the long decay of aristocratic institutions and the rise of egalitarian sentiment. Braudel explains the decline of Mediterranean commerce by attending to the slow rhythms of geography and climate. From these cases one might extract a definition: an adequate historical explanation identifies the immediate causes, places the event within a broader structural context, and is supported by documentary evidence.

Yet this definition, though it captures the structural ambition of the great historians, omits a dimension that each of them regarded as essential. Consider an explanation of the Irish Famine of the 1840s that identifies the immediate cause — the potato blight — and situates it within the structural context of colonial land tenure, monoculture, and chronic poverty. Documentary evidence is abundant. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the explanation omits a factor that many historians regard as indispensable: the role of British government policy. Grain continued to be exported from Ireland while the population starved; relief measures were inadequate; the ideological commitment to laissez-faire economics shaped official responses. These were not structural conditions but decisions made by agents who could have acted otherwise. The background cases all attended, in their different ways, to the agency of the actors — to the decisions, perceptions, and calculations that shaped the course of events within the constraints imposed by structure. The definition has captured the structural and evidential dimensions of explanation but has dropped the agential dimension — the requirement that an adequate explanation account for the choices and actions of the human beings who made history happen.

background_cases: Gibbon's Decline and Fall of the Roman Empire weaves the events of Roman history into a continuous narrative in which each chapter follows from the preceding one, creating a sense of intelligible progression from the Antonine age to the fall of Constantinople.

Macaulay's History of England constructs a narrative in which the Glorious Revolution appears as the culmination of a long struggle between parliamentary liberty and royal prerogative, giving the reader a sense that the events move toward a meaningful resolution.

Burckhardt's Civilisation of the Renaissance in Italy constructs a narrative of cultural awakening in which the arts, politics, and intellectual life of the Italian city-states are woven into a coherent story of the emergence of the modern individual.
definition: An historical account A is a well-formed narrative if and only if (i) A arranges events in a temporal sequence that conveys a sense of development — a beginning, a middle, and an end, (ii) A connects the events through causal, motivational, or thematic links so that each event appears intelligible in relation to what precedes it, and (iii) A employs a consistent point of view or voice throughout.
conditions: A arranges events in a temporal sequence with beginning, middle, and end
A connects events through causal, motivational, or thematic links
A employs a consistent point of view throughout
counterexample: Consider a partisan chronicle of a civil war written by a propagandist for the victorious faction. The chronicle arranges the events in a clear temporal sequence with beginning, middle, and end. It connects the events through causal and motivational links — the enemy's aggression, the righteous response, the inevitable triumph. And it employs a thoroughly consistent point of view. All three conditions are met. Yet the narrative is distorted: events that do not fit the propagandist's story are omitted; the motivations of the losing side are caricatured; and the 'causal' connections are shaped by the desire to justify the victors rather than to understand the events. The narrative is well-formed by the stated criteria but historically worthless.
abductive_insight: The background cases — Gibbon, Macaulay, Burckhardt — all constructed narratives that aspired to fidelity to the available evidence, that considered multiple perspectives, and that acknowledged complexity even while imposing a narrative structure. The definition captures the formal properties of narrative but drops the commitment to evidential fidelity and interpretive honesty that distinguished the great narratives from mere propaganda.
passage:
The great historical narratives — Gibbon on Rome, Macaulay on England, Burckhardt on the Renaissance — share certain formal properties. Each arranges events in a temporal sequence that conveys a sense of development, with a discernible beginning, middle, and end. Each connects events through causal, motivational, or thematic links, so that the reader understands why each event follows from what precedes it. And each maintains a consistent point of view — an authorial voice that guides the reader through the complexity of the material. One might be tempted to define a well-formed historical narrative by these three properties: temporal development, connectedness, and consistency of voice.

Yet these properties, taken alone, do not distinguish historical narrative from propaganda. Consider a partisan chronicle of a civil war, composed by a writer in the service of the victorious faction. The chronicle has a clear temporal arc — the origins of the conflict, the course of the fighting, the glorious triumph. The events are connected by causal and motivational links — the enemy's treachery provoked the righteous response, the courage of the victors ensured the inevitable outcome. The point of view is consistent throughout — relentlessly favourable to the victors, relentlessly hostile to the defeated. All three conditions are met, and yet the result is not history but propaganda. Events that complicate the story are suppressed; the motivations of the losing side are travestied; the causal connections are shaped by the desire to vindicate rather than to understand. The background narratives — Gibbon, Macaulay, Burckhardt — despite their biases, aspired to something the propagandist does not: fidelity to the evidence, willingness to acknowledge complexity, and an honest reckoning with facts that resisted the narrative pattern imposed upon them. The definition has captured the formal architecture of narrative but has dropped the commitment to evidential integrity that distinguishes genuine historical narrative from its counterfeit.

background_cases: The French Revolution of 1789, in which the ancien regime was overthrown by a combination of popular uprising and bourgeois political action, resulting in the establishment of a republic and the Declaration of the Rights of Man.

The Russian Revolution of 1917, in which the Tsarist autocracy was overthrown first by a broad-based popular movement in February and then by a disciplined Bolshevik seizure of power in October, resulting in the establishment of a socialist state.

The Chinese Revolution of 1949, in which the Nationalist government was overthrown by a peasant-based communist movement after decades of civil war, resulting in the establishment of the People's Republic.
definition: An event R constitutes a revolution if and only if (i) R results in the replacement of the existing political order by a fundamentally different one, (ii) R is accomplished through violent means — armed conflict, insurrection, or civil war, and (iii) R brings to power a social class or group that was previously excluded from political authority.
conditions: R replaces the existing political order with a fundamentally different one
R is accomplished through violent means
R brings to power a previously excluded social class or group
counterexample: Consider the Indian independence movement led by Gandhi, which resulted in the replacement of British colonial rule by an independent democratic republic — a fundamentally different political order. The movement brought to power Indian political leaders who had been systematically excluded from authority under British rule. But the decisive means by which independence was achieved were not violent; the movement was characterised by non-violent civil disobedience, mass protest, and political negotiation. The first and third conditions are met, but the second is not, and yet the transformation of India from a colonial possession to an independent republic is widely regarded as revolutionary in its scope and consequences.
abductive_insight: The background cases — France, Russia, China — all involved violence, but the feature that made each revolutionary was not the violence per se but the fundamental character of the political transformation: the old order was not merely reformed but replaced. The definition has taken a feature common to the paradigm cases — violence — and elevated it to a necessary condition, when in fact it was an incidental accompaniment of the deeper structural change.
passage:
The paradigmatic revolutions of modern history — the French Revolution of 1789, the Russian Revolution of 1917, the Chinese Revolution of 1949 — share certain conspicuous features. In each case, an existing political order was replaced by one fundamentally different in character. In each case, the transformation was accomplished by violent means — insurrection, civil war, armed seizure of power. And in each case, a social group previously excluded from political authority was brought to power: the bourgeoisie in France, the proletariat (or its self-appointed representatives) in Russia, the peasantry in China. From these cases one might extract the definition: an event constitutes a revolution if and only if it replaces the political order, is accomplished through violence, and brings a previously excluded group to power.

Yet this definition, shaped by the European and Asian paradigm cases, proves too narrow when applied to the wider history of political transformation. Consider the Indian independence movement. The transformation it achieved was as fundamental as any revolution: British colonial rule was replaced by an independent democratic republic. The group that came to power — the Indian political class, organised through the Indian National Congress — had been systematically excluded from authority under the Raj. But the primary means of transformation were not violent. Gandhi's strategy of non-violent resistance, civil disobedience, and mass mobilisation achieved what armed insurrection had not. The first and third conditions are satisfied, but the second is not. Yet to deny the Indian transformation the title of revolution would be to privilege a particular method over the magnitude and character of the change achieved. The background cases shared the feature of violence, but what made them revolutionary was not the violence itself — it was the depth and completeness of the political transformation. The definition has confused an incidental accompaniment of the paradigm cases with a necessary condition of the concept.

background_cases: The development of antiseptic surgery in the nineteenth century is regarded as progressive because it dramatically reduced mortality from post-operative infection, extending the benefits of surgery to populations that had previously been at grave risk.

The introduction of compulsory elementary education in many European nations during the nineteenth century is regarded as progressive because it extended literacy and basic learning to classes that had previously been denied access to schooling.

The abolition of child labour in the industrialised nations is regarded as progressive because it removed children from dangerous and exploitative conditions and enabled them to receive education and care.
The introduction of clean water and sanitation systems in urban areas is regarded as progressive because it eliminated major sources of epidemic disease and dramatically improved public health.
definition: A change C in a society constitutes progress if and only if (i) C improves the conditions of life for the majority of the population, (ii) C is brought about intentionally by deliberate human action — by policy, legislation, or organised reform, and (iii) C is widely regarded as an improvement at the time it occurs.
conditions: C improves conditions for the majority
C is brought about intentionally by deliberate action
C is widely regarded as an improvement at the time
counterexample: Consider the demographic transition — the shift from high birth and death rates to low birth and death rates — that has occurred across many societies over the past two centuries. The transition has dramatically improved the conditions of life for the majority of the population: lower infant mortality, longer life expectancy, smaller families with more resources per child. Yet the transition was not brought about intentionally by any single act of policy or reform; it emerged from the complex interaction of urbanisation, education, economic development, and changes in cultural norms, none of which were designed to produce the demographic shift. The first condition is met, but the second is not. Yet the demographic transition is one of the most consequential improvements in human welfare in recorded history.
abductive_insight: The background cases — antiseptic surgery, compulsory education, the abolition of child labour, clean water systems — were all brought about by deliberate human action, but the feature that made each progressive was the improvement in human welfare, not the intentionality of the action. The definition has elevated a feature common to the paradigm cases — deliberate reform — to the status of a necessary condition, when in fact the essential feature is the improvement in conditions of life, however brought about.
passage:
When one surveys the changes that are most unambiguously regarded as constituting progress — the development of antiseptic surgery, the introduction of compulsory elementary education, the abolition of child labour, the construction of clean water systems — a common pattern is discernible. Each improved the conditions of life for the majority of the population. Each was brought about by deliberate human action: legislation, organised reform, the application of scientific discovery to practical problems. And each was widely recognised as an improvement at the time it occurred. From these cases one might extract the definition: a change constitutes progress if and only if it improves conditions for the majority, is brought about intentionally, and is widely regarded as an improvement.

Yet this definition, shaped by the paradigm of deliberate reform, proves too narrow when applied to the broader sweep of historical change. Consider the demographic transition — the shift from high birth and death rates to low birth and death rates — that has occurred across much of the world over the past two centuries. The improvement in human welfare has been immense: infant mortality has plummeted, life expectancy has doubled, families have fewer children but are able to invest more in each one. But the demographic transition was not the product of any deliberate policy or reform. No legislator decreed that birth rates should fall; no campaign organised the reduction. The transition emerged from the confluence of urbanisation, rising incomes, female education, and shifting cultural norms — processes that interacted in ways that no single actor planned or foresaw. The first condition is met — conditions have improved for the majority — but the second is not: the improvement was not brought about intentionally. The background cases all happened to involve deliberate reform, but it was not the deliberateness that made them progressive; it was the improvement in human welfare. The definition has confused the mechanism by which the paradigm cases were achieved with the essence of progress itself.

background_cases: The division of European history into 'Antiquity' and 'the Middle Ages,' with the boundary placed at the fall of the Western Roman Empire in 476 CE, reflects a genuine and consequential transformation: the collapse of centralised imperial administration, the decline of urban life, and the fragmentation of political authority.

The division between 'the Middle Ages' and 'the Modern Period,' with the boundary placed variously at the fall of Constantinople in 1453, the discovery of the Americas in 1492, or the Reformation of 1517, reflects a cluster of transformations in political organisation, commerce, and intellectual life.

The division of Chinese history into dynastic periods — Han, Tang, Song, Ming, Qing — reflects genuine changes in political organisation, administrative structure, and cultural production associated with each ruling house.
definition: A periodisation P of a society's history is valid if and only if (i) each period identified by P is characterised by a coherent set of social, political, and cultural features, (ii) the transitions between periods correspond to identifiable events or processes that mark a genuine change, and (iii) P is applicable to all significant domains of the society's life — political, economic, cultural, and intellectual.
conditions: Each period has a coherent set of distinctive features
Transitions correspond to identifiable changes
The periodisation applies to all significant domains of social life
counterexample: Consider the standard periodisation that places the transition from 'Medieval' to 'Early Modern' Europe in the late fifteenth century. In the domain of intellectual life, this periodisation has considerable validity: the revival of classical learning, the development of humanist scholarship, and the new spirit of inquiry that characterise the Renaissance mark a genuine break with the scholastic culture of the preceding period. But in the domain of economic life, the transition is far less clear: the agrarian economy of much of Europe changed only gradually, and the economic structures of the fifteenth century were in many respects continuous with those of the fourteenth. In the domain of political organisation, the picture is similarly mixed: some states modernised rapidly, while others retained medieval structures well into the seventeenth century. The periodisation applies convincingly to intellectual and cultural life but not to economic or political life, violating the third condition. Yet few historians would abandon the concept of the Renaissance merely because economic change did not keep pace with cultural change.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved periodisations that captured genuine and consequential transformations, but none of them applied uniformly across all domains of social life. Dynastic periodisation in Chinese history reflects political change but not necessarily cultural or economic change; the division between Antiquity and the Middle Ages reflects political collapse but not the continuity of religious and intellectual life.
