The definition requires applicability across all domains, but the background cases achieved their value by capturing transformation in the most significant domain, even when other domains changed at different rates. The definition has set a standard of uniformity that the most useful periodisations do not meet.
passage:
The standard periodisations of history — the division of European history into Antiquity, the Middle Ages, and the Modern Period; the dynastic periodisation of Chinese history; the distinction between pre-Columbian and colonial eras in the Americas — share a common ambition. Each seeks to identify stretches of time characterised by a coherent set of features and to mark the transitions between them. From these cases one might extract the definition: a periodisation is valid if and only if each period has coherent distinctive features, the transitions correspond to genuine changes, and the periodisation applies across all significant domains of social life.

Yet this third condition — applicability across all domains — proves far too demanding. Consider the transition from 'Medieval' to 'Early Modern' Europe, conventionally placed in the late fifteenth century. In the domain of intellectual and cultural life, the periodisation is compelling: the revival of classical learning, the development of humanist scholarship, the achievements of Renaissance art mark a genuine transformation. But in the domain of economic life, the transition is much less clear. The agrarian structures of fifteenth-century Europe changed only gradually; the manorial system persisted in many regions; the great economic transformations of capitalism and industrialisation lay centuries in the future. In the domain of political organisation, the picture is similarly uneven: some states centralised power rapidly, while others retained essentially feudal structures well into the seventeenth century. The periodisation captures a transformation in cultural life but not in economic or political life. The third condition is violated. Yet the concept of the Renaissance — and the periodisation it supports — remains one of the most fruitful and illuminating in the historian's vocabulary. The background cases achieved their value not by capturing uniform change across all domains but by identifying the most consequential transformation, even when other domains changed at different rates. The definition demands a synchrony of change that historical reality rarely provides.

background_cases: The decline of the Western Roman Empire in the fifth century is characterised by the progressive loss of territorial control, the breakdown of administrative and legal institutions, the contraction of trade and urban life, and the weakening of the military — a pattern in which each deterioration reinforced the others in a vicious circle.

The decline of the Ottoman Empire in the nineteenth century is characterised by the loss of peripheral territories, the weakening of central authority, the inability to compete economically or militarily with European powers, and the failure of successive reform programmes.

The decline of the Spanish Empire in the seventeenth century is characterised by military overextension, fiscal exhaustion, the loss of commercial supremacy, and the decay of the institutions that had sustained Spain's golden age.
definition: A society S is in decline if and only if (i) the central political institutions of S are losing their capacity to enforce order and administer justice, (ii) the economic productivity of S is falling, and (iii) the military capacity of S has deteriorated to the point where it can no longer defend its territory effectively.
conditions: Central institutions are losing capacity to enforce order
Economic productivity is falling
Military capacity has deteriorated beyond effective territorial defence
counterexample: Consider the Dutch Republic in the early eighteenth century. The Republic's central political institutions — the States-General, the provincial assemblies — had never been particularly strong, and their capacity was, if anything, diminishing as factional disputes and the rivalry between Orangists and Republicans paralysed government. The Republic's share of international trade was declining relative to that of Britain and France. And the Republic's military capacity, so formidable in the seventeenth century, had deteriorated: the army was underfunded, the navy was in disrepair. All three conditions are met. Yet the Dutch Republic in this period was one of the wealthiest, most culturally productive, and most intellectually vibrant societies in Europe. The arts, the sciences, and philosophy flourished; the standard of living remained among the highest in the world. The society was, by the stated criteria, in decline, yet by other measures it was at the height of its civilisation.
abductive_insight: The background cases — Rome, the Ottoman Empire, Spain — involved societies in which institutional, economic, and military decline were accompanied by a broader deterioration in the quality of life and the vitality of civilisation. The definition captures the institutional, economic, and military dimensions but drops the requirement that these indicators reflect a genuine impoverishment of the society's life as a whole. A society may lose political cohesion, economic dominance, and military power while continuing to flourish in other respects.
passage:
The paradigmatic cases of historical decline — the fall of Rome, the weakening of the Ottoman Empire, the decay of Spanish imperial power — share a common structure. In each, the central political institutions lost their capacity to govern effectively; economic productivity contracted; and military capacity deteriorated to the point where the society could no longer defend itself. These three dimensions of decline reinforced one another: fiscal exhaustion weakened the military; military failure eroded political authority; political disorder disrupted economic life. From these cases one might extract the definition: a society is in decline if and only if its institutions are losing capacity, its economy is contracting, and its military can no longer defend its territory.

Yet this definition, abstracted from cases of catastrophic decline, proves insufficient when applied to more nuanced historical situations. Consider the Dutch Republic in the early eighteenth century. The Republic's central institutions were weak and weakening; factional rivalries paralysed the States-General. Its share of international trade was declining as Britain and France overtook it. Its military capacity had deteriorated — the army was underfunded, the navy neglected. All three conditions of the definition are met. Yet the Dutch Republic in this period was, by many measures, one of the most remarkable societies in Europe. Its wealth, though no longer growing as rapidly, remained enormous. Its cultural life was extraordinarily rich. Its scientific and philosophical contributions continued to shape European intellectual life. The society was 'declining' by the stated criteria, yet it was flourishing by others. The background cases involved a more comprehensive deterioration — a decline not merely of institutions, economy, and military but of the society's vitality and quality of life taken as a whole. The definition captures certain measurable indicators of decline but drops the deeper question: whether the society is, taken in the round, impoverished by the changes. A society may lose political cohesion, commercial pre-eminence, and military supremacy while continuing to live well, think boldly, and create abundantly.

background_cases: The legacy of Roman law is visible in the legal systems of modern Europe: the concepts of contract, property, and legal personhood that structure Continental civil law are direct descendants of Roman jurisprudence, transmitted through the medieval study of the Corpus Juris Civilis.

The legacy of the French Revolution is visible in the political vocabulary of modern democracy: the concepts of citizenship, popular sovereignty, and the rights of man that shape modern constitutions descend from the revolutionary declarations and constitutions of the 1790s.

The legacy of the Scientific Revolution is visible in the modern institution of the laboratory, the practice of peer review, and the presupposition that natural phenomena are governed by mathematically expressible laws — all of which descend from the practices and assumptions established in the seventeenth century.
definition: A historical event or movement H has a legacy in a later period if and only if (i) specific features of the later period can be causally traced to H, (ii) the causal chain connecting H to the later features is continuous — there are no gaps in which the influence was entirely absent, and (iii) the later features resemble the original features of H in form or content.
conditions: Features of the later period are causally traceable to H
The causal chain is continuous with no gaps
The later features resemble the original in form or content
counterexample: Consider the legacy of ancient Greek democratic practice in modern representative democracy. Modern democracies are, in an important sense, indebted to the Greek invention of democratic governance. Yet the causal chain is far from continuous. After the fall of Athenian democracy, democratic practice virtually disappeared from Western political life for nearly two millennia. The revival of democratic ideas in the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries drew upon Greek texts, but the texts themselves had been transmitted through Arabic and Byzantine intermediaries, and the democratic ideas they contained had been dormant, not continuously practised. The second condition — continuity of the causal chain — is violated. Yet the debt of modern democracy to ancient Greek practice is real and widely acknowledged.
abductive_insight: The background cases — Roman law, the French Revolution, the Scientific Revolution — all involved continuous chains of transmission, but the essential feature of each legacy was not the continuity of the chain but the causal connection between the original and the later phenomena, however mediated. The definition requires unbroken continuity, but legacies may operate through rediscovery, revival, and reinterpretation across periods of dormancy. The requirement of continuous transmission is too strict.
passage:
The most familiar historical legacies — the influence of Roman law on modern European jurisprudence, the influence of the French Revolution on modern democratic thought, the influence of the Scientific Revolution on modern scientific practice — share a common pattern. In each case, specific features of the later period are causally traceable to the original event or movement. The causal chain is continuous: Roman legal concepts were transmitted through medieval glossators and commentators; revolutionary political ideas were transmitted through constitutions, political movements, and educational institutions; scientific practices were transmitted through the institutional life of universities and learned societies. And the later features resemble the originals in recognisable ways. One might extract the definition: a legacy exists when later features are causally traceable, the causal chain is continuous, and the later features resemble the originals.

Yet this definition, though it describes the paradigm cases well, proves too restrictive. Consider the relationship between ancient Greek democratic practice and modern representative democracy. Modern democratic institutions owe a real debt to the Greek invention: the very idea that the people might govern themselves originated in Athens. But the causal chain is anything but continuous. Athenian democracy was extinguished in the fourth century before Christ. For nearly two thousand years, democratic governance was not practised in the Western world. The revival of democratic ideas in the early modern period drew upon Greek texts, but those texts had been preserved through Arabic translations and Byzantine manuscripts, and the ideas they contained had been dormant — subjects of scholarly curiosity, not living political practice. The second condition is violated: there is a vast gap in the causal chain. Yet the legacy is undeniable. The background cases all involved continuous transmission, but continuity was an incidental feature of those particular cases, not an essential feature of legacy itself. Legacies may operate through rediscovery and revival as well as through continuous practice; the essential feature is the causal connection, however mediated and however interrupted by periods of dormancy.

background_cases: The development of sanitation in Victorian London — the construction of sewers, the provision of clean water — is regarded as progress because it improved health outcomes across all social classes and did not come at the expense of other valued goods.
The abolition of the slave trade in the British Empire is regarded as progress because it expanded the domain of human liberty without imposing a comparable cost on other fundamental values.

The development of anaesthesia in the nineteenth century is regarded as progress because it reduced suffering universally, in a manner that did not exact a toll on other dimensions of human well-being.
definition: A society S has made progress over a period if and only if (i) S has achieved improvements in at least one dimension of human well-being, (ii) these improvements are widely shared among the population, and (iii) the improvements are durable.
conditions: S has achieved improvements in at least one dimension of human well-being
The improvements are widely shared
The improvements are durable
counterexample: Consider the rapid industrialisation of England between 1760 and 1840. There were durable improvements in productive capacity, shared, to varying degrees, across the population. Yet industrialisation also brought urban squalor, child labour, environmental degradation, and the destruction of pre-industrial communities. The improvements in one dimension of well-being — material output — were accompanied by deterioration in others — health, working conditions, and communal life. All three conditions of the definition are formally satisfied with respect to productive capacity, yet to call this period one of unqualified progress seems deeply misleading.
abductive_insight: The background cases — sanitation, abolition, anaesthesia — all involved improvements that did not exact a significant cost in other valued dimensions of human life. The definition captures improvement, breadth, and durability but omits the requirement that progress be net progress — that gains in one dimension not be offset by comparable losses in others.
passage:
When one examines the episodes in history that are most confidently described as progressive, a pattern emerges. The construction of London's sewer system in the 1860s improved health outcomes across social classes without imposing a significant burden on other dimensions of well-being. The abolition of the slave trade expanded human liberty without exacting a comparable cost elsewhere. The development of anaesthesia reduced suffering without introducing new forms of harm. In each case, the improvement was not merely real but unencumbered — it did not come at the price of deterioration in some other domain of human welfare.

One might codify this by defining progress as the achievement of improvements in at least one dimension of human well-being that are widely shared and durable. This definition captures the breadth and persistence of the paradigm cases. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is provided by the early phase of English industrialisation. Between 1760 and 1840, productive capacity grew enormously. The improvements were durable — they were not reversed — and they were shared, to varying degrees, across the population. The three conditions appear to be met. Yet this same period witnessed the growth of urban squalor, the exploitation of child labour on an appalling scale, the degradation of the natural environment, and the dissolution of pre-industrial communal bonds. The gains in material output were real, but they were accompanied by grievous losses in health, dignity, and social cohesion. To call this unqualified progress is to ignore the costs. What the definition omits, and what the background cases all exhibited, is the requirement that gains in one dimension of well-being not be offset by comparable losses in others.

background_cases: The division between the Roman Republic and the Roman Empire is well-founded because the transition from republican to imperial government was marked by a concentration of power in a single ruler, a change that manifested itself across political, military, and administrative domains simultaneously.

The division between the Edo and Meiji periods in Japanese history is well-founded because the Meiji Restoration of 1868 brought simultaneous transformations in government, economy, education, and foreign relations.

The division between pre-war and post-war Europe at 1945 is well-founded because the end of the Second World War brought simultaneous changes in political boundaries, international institutions, economic systems, and social attitudes across the continent.
definition: A periodisation of history is well-founded if and only if (i) the boundaries between periods coincide with events of recognised significance, (ii) the periods are internally coherent in the sense that the events within each period share common features, and (iii) the periods are exhaustive and mutually exclusive.
conditions: Boundaries coincide with significant events
Periods are internally coherent
Periods are exhaustive and mutually exclusive
counterexample: Consider the periodisation of English literary history into 'Augustan' and 'Romantic' periods, with the boundary set around 1785. The boundary coincides with events of literary significance — the death of Johnson, the early publications of Burns and Blake. The periods are internally coherent in the sense that Augustan literature tends toward wit, decorum, and classical models, while Romantic literature tends toward imagination, emotion, and nature. And the division is exhaustive and exclusive. Yet the features attributed to the Romantic period — the celebration of the imagination, the turn to nature, the valorisation of feeling — are already present in mid-eighteenth-century writers such as Thomson, Collins, and Gray. The boundary is far less sharp than the division suggests.
abductive_insight: The background cases — Republic to Empire, Edo to Meiji, pre-war to post-war — all involved transitions that were synchronised across multiple domains of social life. The definition captures significance, coherence, and logical tidiness but omits the requirement that the transition at the boundary be synchronised and relatively abrupt, rather than gradual and spread across decades.
passage:
Periodisations that are widely regarded as well-founded tend to share a distinctive feature. The transition from the Roman Republic to the Empire was marked by changes that manifested simultaneously across political, military, and administrative spheres. The Meiji Restoration brought transformations in government, economy, education, and foreign policy within a few years. The end of the Second World War reshaped political boundaries, international institutions, and social attitudes across an entire continent in short order. In each case, the transition was synchronised — the changes were not spread thinly across decades but concentrated at a recognisable juncture.

One might therefore define a well-founded periodisation as one in which the boundaries coincide with significant events, the periods are internally coherent, and the division is exhaustive and exclusive. This definition captures the logical structure of the paradigm cases. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is provided by the periodisation of English literary history. The conventional division between the Augustan and Romantic periods, with the boundary set around 1785, meets all three conditions: the boundary is marked by significant events (the death of Johnson, the early work of Blake and Burns), the periods are internally coherent (Augustan wit and classical restraint on one side, Romantic imagination and natural feeling on the other), and the division is logically tidy. But the features attributed to the Romantic period — the celebration of the imagination, the reverence for nature, the emphasis upon feeling — did not spring into existence in 1785. They are abundantly present in the work of Thomson, Collins, and Gray in the 1740s and 1750s. The transition was gradual and uneven, not synchronised and abrupt as in the background cases. The definition permits any periodisation that is significant, coherent, and tidy, but the background cases suggest that a further condition is wanted: that the transition at the boundary be concentrated rather than diffused.

background_cases: The decline of the Western Roman Empire in the fifth century is a paradigm case because institutional, military, economic, and cultural deterioration proceeded in rough concert — the legions weakened, the tax base shrank, the cities depopulated, and Latin literary production diminished, all within a recognisable period.

The decline of the Ottoman Empire in the nineteenth century exhibited a similar concurrence — military defeats, territorial contraction, administrative inefficiency, and cultural stagnation reinforced one another.
The decline of the Ming dynasty in the early seventeenth century saw the concurrent deterioration of imperial administration, military capacity, fiscal solvency, and scholarly innovation.
definition: A society S is in decline if and only if (i) the central institutions of S lose their effectiveness, (ii) the material welfare of S's population deteriorates, and (iii) the changes described in (i) and (ii) are sustained over a significant period rather than being momentary disruptions.
conditions: Central institutions lose effectiveness
Material welfare of the population deteriorates
These changes are sustained over a significant period
counterexample: Consider the Weimar Republic in Germany between 1919 and 1933. Central institutions — the Reichstag, the judiciary, the civil service — lost effectiveness as political extremism, economic crisis, and constitutional manipulation undermined them. Material welfare deteriorated sharply, especially during the hyperinflation of 1923 and the Depression after 1929. These deteriorations were sustained over the Republic's fourteen-year life. All three conditions are met. Yet Weimar Germany witnessed one of the most extraordinary cultural flourishings in European history — the Bauhaus, Expressionist cinema, the novels of Mann and Hesse, the music of Weill and Hindemith. The society was in manifest institutional and material decline while simultaneously achieving cultural heights of the first order.
abductive_insight: The background cases — Rome, the Ottomans, the Ming — all exhibited concurrent decline across institutional, material, and cultural dimensions. The definition captures institutional and material decline but omits the cultural dimension. The Weimar case shows that a society may satisfy the definition's conditions while experiencing cultural vitality that complicates the judgement of decline, suggesting that the concept requires attention to all principal dimensions of social life.
passage:
The episodes most confidently characterised as societal decline display a recognisable pattern. In the case of the Western Roman Empire, institutional weakness, military contraction, economic deterioration, and cultural diminution advanced in rough concert. The Ottoman Empire in the nineteenth century exhibited a similar concurrence of military, administrative, and cultural stagnation. The late Ming dynasty saw the simultaneous erosion of fiscal capacity, military strength, administrative competence, and intellectual vitality. In each case, the various dimensions of social life declined together, reinforcing one another.

One might distil from these cases the following definition: a society is in decline if and only if its central institutions lose their effectiveness, the material welfare of its population deteriorates, and these changes are sustained over a significant period. This definition captures the institutional and material dimensions of the paradigm cases and adds a duration requirement to exclude temporary crises. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is the Weimar Republic. Between 1919 and 1933, Germany's central institutions lost their grip: the Reichstag was paralysed by extremist factions, the judiciary was politicised, and constitutional government was progressively undermined. Material welfare collapsed during the hyperinflation of 1923 and again during the Depression. These deteriorations were sustained throughout the Republic's brief existence. All three conditions of the definition are met. Yet this same period produced one of the most remarkable cultural efflorescences in European history — the Bauhaus movement, Expressionist cinema, the literary achievements of Thomas Mann and Hermann Hesse, the musical innovations of Kurt Weill. The background cases all involved decline across every principal dimension of social life, including culture. The definition omits the cultural dimension entirely, and the Weimar case shows that this omission matters: a society satisfying the definition may simultaneously be at the peak of its cultural powers.

background_cases: The legacy of Newton in physics is clear because the effects of his work — the laws of motion, the gravitational theory, the calculus — are recognised by subsequent practitioners as originating from him, and the line of influence is traceable through documented transmission.

The legacy of Adam Smith in political economy is clear because subsequent economists explicitly acknowledged his influence and developed their theories in response to his work, creating a traceable intellectual lineage.

The legacy of Hippocrates in medicine is widely affirmed, though it is complicated by the fact that many works attributed to him were composed by later members of his school; nevertheless, a recognisable line of influence extends from the Hippocratic corpus to later medical practice.
definition: An individual X leaves a legacy in a domain D if and only if (i) X's work produces effects that persist after X's death, (ii) the effects are recognised by subsequent practitioners as traceable to X, and (iii) the line of transmission from X to the subsequent effects is historically documented.
conditions: X's work produces effects persisting after X's death
Effects are recognised as traceable to X
The line of transmission is historically documented
counterexample: Consider the influence of ancient Mesopotamian mathematics on Greek geometry. Babylonian mathematicians developed sophisticated techniques for computing areas, volumes, and what we now call Pythagorean triples, centuries before the Greeks. Greek geometry incorporated many of these results. Yet the line of transmission is almost entirely undocumented — we possess no text in which a Greek mathematician credits a Babylonian source, and the historical route by which the techniques travelled is a matter of scholarly conjecture rather than documentary evidence. The third condition is not met, yet the mathematical legacy appears undeniable.
abductive_insight: The background cases — Newton, Smith, Hippocrates — all involve legacies where the line of transmission is documented through explicit citation, acknowledged discipleship, or identifiable textual dependence. The definition mistakes this documentability for a necessary condition, but the Mesopotamian case shows that influence may be real and consequential even when the line of transmission is historically opaque.
passage:
The legacies most readily affirmed by historians tend to share a feature that invites codification. Newton's legacy in physics is traceable through the explicit acknowledgement of his successors — Euler, Lagrange, Laplace — who built upon his foundations and credited him by name. Adam Smith's legacy in political economy is traceable through the documented responses of Ricardo, Malthus, and Mill, who engaged directly with his texts. Even the legacy of Hippocrates, complicated though it is by questions of authorship, can be traced through the Hippocratic corpus and its documented reception in later antiquity. In each case, the line of transmission is historically visible.

One might therefore define a legacy as the persistent effects of an individual's work that are recognised as traceable to that individual through a historically documented line of transmission. This definition captures the paradigm cases neatly. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is provided by the relationship between ancient Mesopotamian mathematics and Greek geometry. Babylonian mathematicians of the second millennium B.C. developed sophisticated techniques for computing areas and volumes, generated tables of what we now call Pythagorean triples, and employed methods of approximation that reappear in Greek mathematical practice centuries later. The parallels are too numerous and too specific to be coincidental. Yet no Greek mathematical text acknowledges a Babylonian debt. The route of transmission — whether through Phoenician traders, Egyptian intermediaries, or some other channel — is a matter of scholarly hypothesis rather than documentary proof. The third condition of the definition is not met. But to deny that Babylonian mathematics left a legacy to Greek geometry seems historically untenable. The definition confuses the visibility of the transmission with its reality, requiring documentation where influence may have operated through channels that left no written trace.

background_cases: The explanation of the fall of the Roman Republic by appeal to the concentration of military power in the hands of individual commanders — Marius, Sulla, Pompey, Caesar — succeeds because it identifies a structural change that made the traditional constitutional arrangements unworkable, and the explanation can be tested against the comparative evidence of other republics that underwent similar militarisation.

The explanation of the French Revolution by appeal to the fiscal crisis of the Bourbon monarchy succeeds because it identifies a proximate cause that made the summoning of the Estates-General necessary, and the explanation can be assessed against the evidence of other fiscal crises that did or did not produce revolutionary outcomes.

The explanation of the Industrial Revolution by appeal to the availability of cheap coal and the development of patent law in England succeeds because it identifies specific enabling conditions and can be tested by asking why industrialisation occurred in England rather than in countries that lacked these conditions.
definition: A historical explanation of an event E is adequate if and only if (i) it identifies conditions that were necessary for E to occur, (ii) the conditions are supported by documentary evidence, and (iii) the explanation is consistent with the known facts about E.
conditions: It identifies necessary conditions for E
The conditions are supported by documentary evidence
The explanation is consistent with known facts about E
counterexample: Consider the explanation of the outbreak of the First World War by appeal to the alliance system, the arms race, imperial rivalries, and nationalist sentiment. Each of these conditions is supported by ample documentary evidence, and the explanation is consistent with the known facts. One might argue that each condition was necessary: without the alliance system, a Balkan crisis need not have drawn in the Great Powers; without the arms race, the Powers might have been less prepared for war; and so forth. All three conditions of the definition are met. Yet the same conditions — alliance systems, arms races, imperial rivalries, nationalist sentiment — had existed for decades before 1914 without producing a general war. The explanation identifies the background conditions but does not explain why the war broke out in August 1914 rather than at some earlier or later date. It lacks what one might call a triggering account — an explanation of why these long-standing conditions produced their effect at this particular moment.
abductive_insight: The background cases all identify not merely necessary background conditions but specific triggering factors or structural changes that explain why the event occurred when it did: the concentration of military power in the hands of ambitious commanders, the specific fiscal crisis of 1789, the specific combination of coal and patent law in eighteenth-century England. The definition requires only that necessary conditions be identified, but the paradigm cases show that an adequate explanation must also account for the timing and specificity of the event — why these conditions produced this outcome at this moment.
passage:
The historical explanations most widely regarded as successful share a feature that the philosophy of history has not always made explicit. The explanation of the fall of the Roman Republic identifies not merely the general conditions of late republican politics — the ambition of senators, the restiveness of the populace — but the specific structural change that made the old constitution unworkable: the concentration of military power in the hands of individual commanders who could use their legions against the state. The explanation of the French Revolution identifies not merely the general discontents of the ancien regime but the specific fiscal crisis that forced the King to summon the Estates-General. The explanation of the Industrial Revolution identifies not merely the existence of technological know-how but the specific combination of cheap coal and patent protection that obtained in England and not elsewhere. In each case, the explanation accounts not only for the occurrence of the event but for its timing and location.

One might codify these cases by defining an adequate historical explanation as one that identifies necessary conditions, supported by documentary evidence, that are consistent with the known facts. This definition captures the evidential and logical requirements of the paradigm cases. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is the explanation of the outbreak of the First World War. The standard account identifies the alliance system, the arms race, imperial rivalries, and nationalist sentiment as necessary conditions. Each is amply documented. The explanation is consistent with the known facts. All three conditions of the definition are met. But these same conditions — alliance systems, arms races, imperial competition, nationalism — had obtained for decades before 1914. Why did they produce a general European war in August 1914 and not in the Moroccan crisis of 1905, or the Bosnian crisis of 1908, or at any other point during the preceding forty years? The definition permits an explanation that identifies the standing conditions without accounting for the precipitating event — the assassination at Sarajevo — and the specific sequence of decisions and miscalculations that transformed a Balkan crisis into a world war. The background cases all explained not only that the event occurred but why it occurred when and where it did. The definition omits this requirement.

background_cases: Thucydides' account of the Peloponnesian War is regarded as a model of historical narrative because the narrator effaces himself, presents the evidence, and allows the reader to weigh the testimony of the participants without being directed towards a predetermined conclusion.

Ranke's ideal of telling history 'as it actually happened' is regarded as a model because it requires the historian to subordinate personal judgement to the evidence of the sources, producing a narrative whose authority derives from its fidelity to the documentary record.

The Annales school's emphasis on quantitative social history is valued because it grounds historical narrative in statistical evidence — demographic records, price series, tax rolls — that constrain the historian's interpretive freedom and lend the narrative an objectivity that literary history lacks.
definition: A historical narrative N is objective if and only if (i) N is based exclusively on primary sources, (ii) N does not reflect the personal values or political commitments of the narrator, and (iii) N presents events in a manner that any competent historian, given the same sources, would endorse.
conditions: N is based exclusively on primary sources
N does not reflect the narrator's personal values or politics
Any competent historian with the same sources would endorse N
counterexample: Consider two equally competent historians given the same primary sources concerning the British Empire in India. One, focusing on the documentary record of railway construction, public health improvements, and legal codification, produces a narrative of modernisation and development. The other, focusing with equal diligence on the documentary record of famine, land dispossession, and cultural suppression, produces a narrative of exploitation and destruction. Both narratives are based exclusively on primary sources. Both historians may sincerely believe that their personal values have not influenced their account. Yet the narratives are irreconcilable, and a third historian given the same sources would not necessarily endorse either. The choice of which sources to foreground and which to set aside is itself a judgement that reflects values, even when the historian is unaware of making it.
abductive_insight: The background cases — Thucydides, Ranke, the Annales school — all aspire to objectivity by constraining the narrator's freedom: Thucydides effaces himself, Ranke defers to the sources, the Annales school relies on statistics. The definition captures these constraints but fails to recognise that the selection and weighting of sources is itself an interpretive act that reflects the historian's values and perspectives, however sincerely the historian may believe in his or her own objectivity.
passage:
The ideals of historical objectivity most often invoked share a common aspiration: the subordination of the historian's personal judgement to the evidence. Thucydides presents speeches and actions without editorial commentary, allowing the reader to judge. Ranke's dictum — to show what actually happened — demands that the historian serve the sources rather than impose upon them. The Annales school grounds its narratives in quantitative evidence that resists subjective manipulation. In each case, objectivity is pursued by constraining the historian's interpretive freedom.

One might codify this aspiration by defining an objective historical narrative as one based exclusively on primary sources, free from the narrator's personal values, and endorsable by any competent historian given the same evidence. This definition captures the spirit of the paradigm cases. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency concerns the history of the British Empire in India. Suppose two equally competent, equally honest historians are given the same archive of primary sources. The first, drawn to the documentary record of railway construction, sanitation projects, and legal reform, produces a narrative of modernisation. The second, drawn with equal scholarly integrity to the documentary record of famine deaths, forced land transfers, and the suppression of indigenous institutions, produces a narrative of exploitation. Both narratives are based exclusively on primary sources. Both historians may sincerely believe that they have excluded their personal values from the account. Yet the narratives are deeply incompatible, and a third historian, given the same archive, might produce a third narrative different from both. The definition assumes that the sources determine the narrative, but the archive is vast and heterogeneous, and the act of selecting which sources to foreground — the railway ledgers or the famine mortality records — is itself a judgement that reflects values and commitments, however unconsciously. The background cases pursued objectivity by constraining interpretation, but the act of selection, which precedes interpretation, is itself shaped by the very values the definition seeks to exclude.

background_cases: The French Revolution of 1789 is a paradigm case because it involved the rapid, violent overthrow of an established political order and its replacement by a fundamentally different one — the Bourbon monarchy gave way to a republic within four years, and the transformation was effected by popular uprising and armed conflict.

The Russian Revolution of 1917 is a paradigm case because the Tsarist autocracy was overthrown by armed insurrection and replaced within months by a Bolshevik state committed to a radically different political and economic programme.

The Chinese Revolution of 1949 is a paradigm case because the Nationalist government was overthrown by armed communist forces and replaced by a state committed to the wholesale transformation of Chinese society and economy.
definition: An event R is a revolution if and only if (i) R replaces the existing political order with a fundamentally different one, (ii) the replacement is effected by the mobilisation of large numbers of people outside the established political institutions, and (iii) the replacement is accomplished rapidly — within a period of months or a few years at most.
conditions: R replaces the existing political order with a fundamentally different one
The replacement is effected by mass mobilisation outside established institutions
The replacement is accomplished rapidly
counterexample: Consider the Meiji Restoration of 1868 in Japan. The Tokugawa shogunate was replaced by a government committed to the radical transformation of Japanese society — the abolition of the feudal domains, the creation of a conscript army, the adoption of Western technology and institutions, the rewriting of the legal code. The political order was fundamentally replaced. Yet the transformation was not effected by mass mobilisation outside established institutions; it was engineered by a coalition of samurai from a small number of domains — Satsuma, Choshu, Tosa, Hizen — working through and ultimately capturing the institution of the Emperor. The masses were largely spectators. Condition (ii) is not met, yet the Meiji Restoration is widely regarded by historians as one of the most consequential revolutions in modern history.
abductive_insight: The background cases — France, Russia, China — all involve mass popular mobilisation as the vehicle of revolutionary change. The definition captures this feature as a necessary condition. But the Meiji case shows that a fundamental and rapid transformation of the political order may be effected by a small elite operating within or through existing institutions, without significant mass mobilisation. The definition mistakes the European and Chinese model of revolution — popular uprising from below — for a universal condition, when the Meiji case demonstrates that revolution may also take the form of radical transformation from above.
passage:
The revolutions most frequently cited as paradigmatic in the Western historical tradition share a distinctive sociological feature. The French Revolution was driven by the mobilisation of the Parisian populace — the storming of the Bastille, the march on Versailles, the sans-culottes in the streets. The Russian Revolution was driven by the mobilisation of soldiers, workers, and peasants who had been radicalised by war and deprivation. The Chinese Revolution was driven by the mobilisation of a vast peasant army under communist leadership. In each case, the revolutionary transformation was accomplished by the mass entry of ordinary people into political action outside the established institutions of the state.

One might codify these cases by defining a revolution as an event that replaces the existing political order with a fundamentally different one, through the rapid mobilisation of large numbers of people outside established institutions. This definition captures the paradigm cases faithfully. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is the Meiji Restoration of 1868. The Tokugawa shogunate, which had governed Japan for over two and a half centuries, was replaced by a government that embarked upon one of the most thoroughgoing transformations in modern history — the abolition of feudal domains, the creation of a modern army, the adoption of Western legal and educational institutions, the reorganisation of the economy. The political order was fundamentally replaced, and the replacement was accomplished with remarkable speed. But the transformation was not effected by mass mobilisation. It was engineered by a small coalition of samurai from four domains, who exploited the symbolic authority of the Emperor to legitimise their seizure of power. The common people of Japan were largely uninvolved in the political events of 1868. The definition, built from the European model of popular revolution, cannot accommodate a case in which a small elite, operating through existing institutions, accomplishes a transformation no less radical than those achieved by mass mobilisation.

background_cases: The common law tradition in England is regarded as a genuine tradition because it has been transmitted continuously from generation to generation of jurists, each of whom received it from predecessors and passed it on with modifications that were understood as developments of an existing body of practice rather than innovations.

The tradition of classical music in the Western conservatory is regarded as a genuine tradition because the techniques, repertoire, and aesthetic values are transmitted from teacher to pupil in an unbroken chain, with each generation understanding itself as the custodian of an inherited art.

The tradition of Confucian scholarship in China is regarded as a genuine tradition because the canonical texts, the methods of commentary, and the ethical ideals have been transmitted from master to disciple across millennia, with each generation understanding itself as the faithful steward of an ancient teaching.
definition: A practice P constitutes a tradition if and only if (i) P has been transmitted across at least three generations, (ii) the practitioners of P understand themselves as continuing what was handed down to them, and (iii) the content of P — its beliefs, practices, or techniques — has remained recognisably continuous throughout the period of transmission.
conditions: P has been transmitted across at least three generations
Practitioners understand themselves as continuing an inherited practice
The content of P has remained recognisably continuous
counterexample: Consider the 'tradition' of Highland dress in Scotland — the tartan kilt, clan-specific patterns, and associated regalia. The practitioners regard themselves as continuing an ancient Highland tradition. The content — the wearing of tartan in clan-specific patterns — appears recognisably continuous. Yet historical research has shown that much of what passes for ancient tradition was largely invented in the late eighteenth and early nineteenth centuries, particularly after the romanticisation of the Highlands by Walter Scott and the visit of George IV to Edinburgh in 1822. Clan-specific tartans were largely the creation of commercial weavers in the 1820s and 1830s. The practice has been transmitted across more than three generations since its invention. The practitioners sincerely believe they are continuing an ancient tradition. The content is recognisably continuous. All three conditions are met — yet the tradition is, in large part, a modern invention with shallow historical roots.
abductive_insight: The background cases — common law, classical music, Confucian scholarship — all involve traditions whose claimed antiquity is historically genuine: the continuity extends back as far as the tradition claims. The definition captures transmission, self-understanding, and continuity of content, but it does not require that the tradition's self-understanding of its own antiquity be historically accurate. An invented tradition may satisfy all three conditions while resting on a false narrative of its own origins.
passage:
The traditions that are most securely regarded as genuine share a cluster of features that invite generalisation. The common law of England has been transmitted from generation to generation of jurists across centuries, each generation receiving a body of precedent and principle from its predecessors and passing it on, modified but recognisable, to its successors. The tradition of Western classical music has been transmitted from teacher to pupil in conservatories across Europe, with techniques, repertoire, and aesthetic values handed down in an unbroken chain. The Confucian scholarly tradition has transmitted canonical texts, methods of commentary, and ethical ideals from master to disciple across millennia. In each case, the tradition's antiquity is genuine: the continuity extends back as far as the tradition claims.

One might codify these cases by defining a tradition as a practice transmitted across at least three generations, whose practitioners understand themselves as continuing what was handed down to them, and whose content has remained recognisably continuous. This definition captures the transmission, self-understanding, and continuity evident in the paradigm cases. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is the 'tradition' of Highland dress in Scotland. The tartan kilt, the clan-specific patterns, and the associated regalia are understood by their practitioners as the continuation of an ancient Highland tradition. The content has been transmitted across more than three generations and has remained recognisably continuous since the early nineteenth century. The practitioners sincerely regard themselves as custodians of an inherited practice. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied. But historical scholarship — most notably the work of Hugh Trevor-Roper — has demonstrated that much of this tradition was invented in the late eighteenth and early nineteenth centuries. Clan-specific tartans were largely the creation of commercial weavers in the 1820s. The romanticised Highland dress owes more to Walter Scott than to any genuinely ancient practice. The tradition is real in the sense that it has been transmitted and is sincerely practised, but its self-understanding of its own antiquity is historically false. The background cases all involved traditions whose claimed antiquity was genuine. The definition permits a tradition to satisfy all conditions while resting on a fabricated origin story.

background_cases: Thucydides' account of the Peloponnesian War is regarded as a model of historical narrative because it explains the course of events by reference to the rational deliberations and strategic calculations of the principal agents — Pericles, Cleon, Alcibiades — and one grasps why events unfolded as they did by understanding what these agents intended and why.

Gibbon's narrative of the decline of the Roman Empire explains the transformation of the ancient world by attending to the decisions of emperors, generals, and ecclesiastics, and the reader understands the decline by understanding the motives, errors, and deliberate choices of the agents whose actions produced it.

Ranke's account of the wars of religion in early modern Europe explains the course of the Reformation by attending to the political calculations and theological convictions of princes and reformers, and the explanatory force of the narrative derives from the intelligibility of the agents' reasons for acting as they did.
definition: An account N constitutes an adequate historical narrative if and only if (i) N explains the course of events by reference to the intentions, reasons, and decisions of the agents involved, (ii) N is based on primary-source evidence, and (iii) N presents the events in a coherent temporal sequence.
conditions: N explains events by reference to the intentions, reasons, and decisions of agents
N is based on primary-source evidence
N presents events in a coherent temporal sequence
counterexample: Consider a narrative of the depopulation of fourteenth-century Europe that explains the catastrophic mortality by reference to the bubonic plague bacterium, the role of rat-borne fleas in transmission, and the immunological vulnerability of a population with no prior exposure to the pathogen. The narrative is based on primary-source evidence — parish records, chronicles, wills — and presents events in temporal sequence. Yet the central explanatory factors are not the intentions or decisions of agents but biological and epidemiological processes that operated independently of human deliberation. No amount of attention to the reasons and decisions of fourteenth-century Europeans will explain why the death rate was so high.
abductive_insight: The background cases all concern sequences of events that were shaped primarily by human deliberation — wars, political decisions, reforms — where the agents' reasons are the principal explanatory factors. The definition codifies this agent-centred pattern as a universal requirement. Yet the plague narrative reveals that some of the most important historical events are shaped by impersonal forces — biological, environmental, demographic — that operate independently of human intention. The definition mistakes a feature of one important genre of historical explanation for a necessary condition of adequate narrative.
passage:
The greatest works of historical narrative share a distinctive explanatory structure. Thucydides explains the Peloponnesian War by disclosing the strategic calculations of Pericles, Cleon, and Alcibiades. Gibbon explains the decline of Rome by attending to the decisions of emperors and ecclesiastics. Ranke explains the Reformation by tracing the political and theological reasoning of princes and reformers. In each case, the narrative's explanatory force derives from the intelligibility of the agents' reasons for acting as they did.

One might codify this pattern by the following definition: an account constitutes an adequate historical narrative if and only if it explains events by reference to the intentions, reasons, and decisions of the agents involved, is based on primary-source evidence, and presents events in a coherent temporal sequence. This definition captures the agent-centred character of the paradigmatic narratives. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when one considers the demographic catastrophe of fourteenth-century Europe. An adequate narrative of the Black Death must explain why the population of Europe fell by a third or more within a few years. The explanation lies not in the intentions or decisions of any human agent but in the biology of Yersinia pestis, the ecology of its rat and flea vectors, and the immunological vulnerability of a population encountering the pathogen for the first time. The narrative may be based upon primary sources — parish mortality records, contemporary chronicles, testamentary evidence — and it may present events in impeccable temporal sequence. Yet its explanatory core is impersonal: biological and epidemiological processes that no human agent intended, decided, or could have controlled. One might observe that the definition, by requiring explanation through agents' reasons, fails to accommodate the large class of historical events whose causes are impersonal.

background_cases: The division between the Roman Republic and the Roman Empire is marked by a series of specific, datable events — the crossing of the Rubicon, the battle of Actium, the assumption of the title Augustus — that represent a genuine discontinuity in the political structure of the Roman state; the boundary between the periods corresponds to a rapid and identifiable transformation.

The division between the Ancien Regime and revolutionary France is marked by the events of 1789 — the storming of the Bastille, the abolition of feudal privileges, the Declaration of the Rights of Man — that represent a sudden and unmistakable rupture in the political and social order; the boundary is clear because the transformation was swift.

The division between the Tokugawa and Meiji periods in Japanese history is marked by the Restoration of 1868 and the rapid programme of modernisation that followed; the boundary corresponds to a concentrated series of institutional changes that transformed Japanese society within a few decades.
definition: A periodisation of the past is historically legitimate if and only if (i) each period is characterised by a coherent set of distinctive features, (ii) the boundaries between periods correspond to identifiable turning points in the historical record, and (iii) the periodisation illuminates significant patterns or developments that would be obscured without it.
conditions: Each period is characterised by a coherent set of distinctive features
Boundaries correspond to identifiable turning points in the historical record
The periodisation illuminates significant patterns or developments
counterexample: Consider the periodisation of Chinese history into dynastic periods — Han, Tang, Song, Ming, Qing, and so forth. Each dynasty is characterised by distinctive political, cultural, and economic features. The boundaries correspond to identifiable turning points — the fall of one dynasty and the rise of the next. The periodisation illuminates important political patterns. All three conditions are met. Yet the dynastic periodisation obscures continuities that cut across dynastic boundaries — the gradual commercialisation of the economy from the Song through the Ming, the slow southward shift of the demographic centre of gravity, the centuries-long development of Neo-Confucian philosophy. The periodisation's boundaries, though real as political events, impose artificial ruptures upon processes that were continuous.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involve periods whose boundaries correspond not merely to political events but to transformations that were pervasive across multiple dimensions of social life — political, economic, cultural. The definition requires only identifiable turning points, without specifying that the turning points must represent transformations across multiple domains. The dynastic case reveals that a boundary may correspond to a genuine political turning point whilst obscuring the continuity of deeper social, economic, and intellectual processes.
passage:
The most illuminating periodisations of the past tend to mark boundaries that correspond to transformations spanning multiple dimensions of social life. The transition from Republic to Empire in Rome involved not merely a change of political form but a transformation of military organisation, cultural patronage, and provincial administration. The French Revolution of 1789 ruptured not only the political order but the social hierarchy, the legal system, and the framework of economic privileges. The Meiji Restoration transformed not only the Japanese state but the educational system, the military, and the economy within a generation.

One might attempt to codify the conditions for legitimate periodisation as follows: a periodisation is historically legitimate if and only if each period is characterised by a coherent set of distinctive features, the boundaries correspond to identifiable turning points, and the periodisation illuminates significant patterns. This definition captures the merits of the paradigmatic periodisations. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is the division of Chinese history into dynastic periods. Each dynasty — Han, Tang, Song, Ming, Qing — is characterised by distinctive political and cultural features. The boundaries correspond to identifiable turning points: the fall of one dynasty and the rise of the next are datable events of profound political significance. The periodisation illuminates important patterns of political rise and decline. Yet the dynastic framework obscures continuities of equal or greater significance. The gradual commercialisation of the Chinese economy, which began in the Song period and continued through the Ming, is artificially interrupted by the dynastic boundary. The centuries-long development of Neo-Confucian thought cuts across multiple dynasties. One might observe that the definition, by requiring only identifiable turning points, permits boundaries that correspond to genuine political ruptures whilst concealing the continuity of deeper historical processes.

background_cases: The explanation of why Britain industrialised before other European nations cites a conjunction of factors — abundant coal and iron, navigable waterways, a tradition of mechanical ingenuity, a legal framework favourable to commerce — no one of which was sufficient but whose conjunction made industrialisation possible; the explanation works by identifying the set of conditions whose co-occurrence was necessary.

The explanation of why the Roman Republic gave way to autocracy cites a conjunction of factors — the professionalisation of the army, the extension of citizenship, the concentration of wealth, the inadequacy of republican institutions for governing an empire — whose convergence produced a situation that only autocratic rule could resolve; the explanation identifies the structural conditions that made the transformation possible.

The explanation of why the Scientific Revolution occurred in seventeenth-century Europe cites a conjunction of factors — the recovery of Greek texts, the development of mathematical techniques, the invention of instruments like the telescope and microscope, the emergence of institutions for the communication of results — whose convergence created the conditions for a new mode of inquiry.
definition: An account H constitutes an adequate historical explanation of an event E if and only if (i) H identifies a set of conditions jointly sufficient for the occurrence of E, (ii) H shows that these conditions obtained prior to E, and (iii) H is supported by documentary evidence.
conditions: H identifies a set of conditions jointly sufficient for E
H shows that these conditions obtained prior to E
H is supported by documentary evidence
counterexample: Consider an explanation of why the First World War began in August 1914. One might identify a set of jointly sufficient conditions — the alliance system, the arms race, imperial rivalries, nationalist sentiment, the domestic political pressures on the various governments — and show that all of these conditions obtained prior to August 1914. The explanation is well supported by documentary evidence. All three conditions are met. Yet these very conditions had obtained for years, even decades, before 1914. The alliance system was in place by the 1890s; the arms race had been under way since the turn of the century; imperial rivalries and nationalist sentiment were of long standing. The explanation fails to account for why the war began when it did — in August 1914 rather than at any earlier moment when the same set of conditions obtained.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involve explanations of events whose timing is not sharply puzzling — industrialisation, the fall of the Republic, and the Scientific Revolution were gradual processes rather than events with a precise date. The definition codifies a pattern suited to such processes. But the outbreak of the First World War has a precise date, and the jointly sufficient conditions cited in the explanation had obtained for years without producing war. What is missing is an account of the triggering event or precipitating cause that converted the standing conditions into actual conflict at a particular moment.
passage:
Many of the most successful historical explanations work by identifying a conjunction of conditions whose convergence made a given development possible. Britain industrialised because it possessed coal, waterways, mechanical traditions, and favourable legal institutions. The Roman Republic gave way to autocracy because the army was professionalised, wealth was concentrated, and republican institutions could not govern an empire. The Scientific Revolution occurred because Greek texts were recovered, mathematical techniques were developed, instruments were invented, and learned societies were established. In each case, the explanation identifies the set of conditions that, taken together, were sufficient for the outcome.

One might propose the following definition: an account constitutes an adequate historical explanation if and only if it identifies a set of conditions jointly sufficient for the event, shows that these conditions obtained prior to the event, and is supported by documentary evidence. This definition captures the explanatory structure of the paradigmatic cases. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is the outbreak of the First World War. One may identify the alliance system, the arms race, imperial rivalries, nationalist sentiment, and the domestic political pressures on the European governments as a set of conditions jointly sufficient for a general European war. Documentary evidence confirms that all of these conditions obtained prior to August 1914. Yet these very conditions had obtained for years — in some cases, for decades — without producing war. The alliance system was in place by the 1890s; the naval arms race began before the turn of the century. The explanation identifies the conditions that made war possible but fails to explain why war broke out when it did. What is needed, and what the definition does not require, is an account of the precipitating cause — the assassination at Sarajevo, the Austrian ultimatum, the mobilisation decisions of July 1914 — that converted standing conditions into actual conflict.

background_cases: The English Revolution of the 1640s overthrew the monarchy, established a republic, and was followed by the restoration of the Stuart kings in 1660; the revolutionary settlement was reversed, but the revolution itself — the trial and execution of the king, the abolition of the House of Lords, the establishment of the Commonwealth — was a genuine upheaval of the political order.

The French Revolution overthrew the Bourbon monarchy and the feudal order, and although Napoleon subsequently established an empire and the Bourbons were eventually restored, the revolution itself effected a genuine transformation — the abolition of feudal privileges, the Declaration of Rights, the reorganisation of the state — that could not be fully reversed.

The Russian Revolution of 1917 overthrew the Tsarist autocracy and established a socialist state, and while the Soviet system eventually collapsed in 1991, the revolution itself was a genuine and radical transformation of the political, economic, and social order.
definition: An event R constitutes a genuine revolution if and only if (i) R effects a rapid and fundamental transformation of the political order, (ii) R is accompanied by widespread social upheaval, and (iii) R produces a new political settlement that endures.
conditions: R effects a rapid and fundamental transformation of the political order
R is accompanied by widespread social upheaval
R produces a new political settlement that endures
counterexample: Consider the Hungarian Revolution of 1956. The uprising effected a rapid transformation of the political order — the communist government was overthrown, political prisoners were released, and a multi-party system was briefly established. The event was accompanied by widespread social upheaval — workers' councils were formed across the country, street fighting engulfed Budapest, and the population mobilised against Soviet domination. Conditions (i) and (ii) were clearly met. Yet the revolution was crushed by Soviet military intervention within two weeks, and the pre-revolutionary communist order was reimposed. The revolution did not produce an enduring new settlement. On the definition's account, the Hungarian Revolution was not a genuine revolution, which seems wrong.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involve revolutions that produced lasting settlements, but a closer examination reveals that their revolutionary character was established by the upheaval itself — the execution of the king, the abolition of feudal privileges, the overthrow of the Tsar — regardless of what followed. The definition mistakes the durability of consequences for a constitutive feature of revolution, when the essential character of a revolution lies in the transformative act itself rather than in the longevity of its political outcome.
passage:
The paradigmatic revolutions in modern history share certain features that tempt one toward a general definition. The English Revolution overthrew the monarchy and established a republic. The French Revolution abolished the feudal order and proclaimed the rights of man. The Russian Revolution ended the Tsarist autocracy and created a socialist state. In each case, a rapid and fundamental transformation of the political order was accompanied by widespread social upheaval, and the new settlement, though modified over time, proved broadly enduring.

One might codify these features in the following definition: an event constitutes a genuine revolution if and only if it effects a rapid and fundamental transformation of the political order, is accompanied by widespread social upheaval, and produces a new political settlement that endures. This definition captures the essential features of the paradigmatic cases. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is the Hungarian Revolution of 1956. In October of that year, the Hungarian people rose against the Soviet-backed communist government. Within days, the political order was transformed: the communist government fell, political prisoners were released, workers' councils were established, and a multi-party system was declared. The upheaval was as widespread as any in modern history — the entire population of Budapest was mobilised, and workers' councils appeared in cities across the country. Conditions (i) and (ii) were met beyond question. Yet Soviet tanks entered Budapest on the fourth of November, and within two weeks the revolution was crushed and the old order reimposed. No enduring new settlement was produced. One might observe that it would be deeply counterintuitive to deny that the Hungarian event was a revolution, and that the definition, by requiring endurance, mistakes a contingent feature of successful revolutions for a necessary condition of revolution as such.

background_cases: The explanation of the collapse of the Western Roman Empire typically cites not only the military pressures from Germanic tribes but also the internal fiscal crisis, the loss of civic solidarity, and the failure of political leadership — a convergence of factors none of which alone would have been sufficient.

The explanation of the Industrial Revolution in Britain cites not only the availability of coal and iron but also the development of property rights, the culture of practical invention, and the existence of a mobile labour force — a conjunction of conditions that was unique to eighteenth-century Britain.

The explanation of the fall of the Bastille in 1789 cites not only the political crisis of the Estates-General but also the food shortage, the fear of royal repression, and the symbolic significance of the fortress — a constellation of factors that made the event possible and intelligible.
definition: An account A constitutes a satisfactory historical explanation of an event E if and only if (i) A identifies a cause or set of causes that, taken together, are sufficient for E, (ii) the causes cited in A are supported by documentary evidence, and (iii) A arranges the causes in a temporal sequence that makes the occurrence of E intelligible.
conditions: A identifies a cause or set of causes that, taken together, are sufficient for E
The causes cited in A are supported by documentary evidence
A arranges the causes in a temporal sequence that makes the occurrence of E intelligible
counterexample: Consider an explanation of the outbreak of the English Civil War that cites the religious tensions between Puritans and Anglicans, the fiscal disputes between Crown and Parliament, and the personal obstinacy of Charles I — all supported by abundant documentary evidence and arranged in a temporal sequence that makes the outbreak of war intelligible. All three conditions are met. Yet one might observe that the same religious tensions, fiscal disputes, and royal stubbornness were present in comparable measure in the decades before the Civil War, during which no war occurred. The cited causes were sufficient in 1642 only because of a particular conjuncture of contingent events — the Irish rebellion, the Army Plot — that the explanation does not identify.
abductive_insight: The background cases reveal that satisfactory historical explanations characteristically identify not merely the long-term structural causes but also the precipitating conjuncture of contingent events that transformed latent tensions into actual outcomes. The definition captures the structural dimension of explanation but omits the requirement that the account identify the specific conjuncture — the 'trigger' or catalytic event — that activated the otherwise dormant sufficient conditions.
passage:
When one examines the most compelling historical explanations, a pattern emerges that goes beyond the identification of sufficient causes. The explanation of Rome's fall cites not only structural pressures but the specific conjuncture in which fiscal crisis, military defeat, and political failure converged. The explanation of the Industrial Revolution identifies not only the availability of raw materials but the unique conjuncture of institutions, culture, and labour that made Britain's transformation possible. The explanation of the storming of the Bastille traces not only the political crisis but the specific confluence of hunger, fear, and symbolism that precipitated action.

One might attempt to codify this explanatory practice with the following definition: an account constitutes a satisfactory historical explanation if it identifies causes sufficient for the event, supports them with documentary evidence, and arranges them in an intelligible temporal sequence. This definition captures much of what historians aim to provide. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises with the English Civil War. An historian might cite the religious tensions between Puritans and Anglicans, the fiscal disputes between Crown and Parliament, and the personal intransigence of Charles I — all abundantly documented and temporally sequenced. The conditions appear sufficient for civil war. Yet one might observe that these very tensions persisted for decades before 1642 without producing armed conflict. What was different in 1642 was the specific conjuncture of the Irish rebellion, the Army Plot, and the collapse of trust between King and Commons — precipitating events that transformed latent tensions into actual warfare. The explanation, by citing only the long-term structural causes, fails to identify the conjuncture that activated them. It appears that the definition, by requiring only sufficiency, cannot distinguish an account of why war was possible from an account of why it broke out when it did.

background_cases: Thucydides' history of the Peloponnesian War achieved its explanatory power not merely by recounting what happened but by adopting the perspective of the principal actors — Pericles, Cleon, Nicias — and rendering their deliberations intelligible; the narrative explained by making the agents' reasoning accessible to the reader.

Gibbon's Decline and Fall of the Roman Empire achieved its authority not merely by chronicling events but by adopting a perspective — secular, ironic, detached — that allowed the reader to perceive patterns invisible to the participants themselves; the narrative illuminated by standing above the events.

Macaulay's History of England adopted the perspective of the Whig tradition, interpreting the Glorious Revolution as a triumph of constitutional liberty; the narrative derived its coherence from a perspective that selected and arranged events according to a governing theme.
definition: A historical narrative N is satisfactory if and only if (i) N recounts the events in their correct temporal order, (ii) N is faithful to the documentary evidence, and (iii) N connects the events by identifying the causal relations among them.
conditions: N recounts the events in their correct temporal order
N is faithful to the documentary evidence
N connects the events by identifying the causal relations among them
counterexample: Consider two narratives of the same war, both recounting the events in correct temporal order, both faithful to the documentary evidence, and both identifying the same causal relations among events. The first narrative is written from the perspective of the victors, emphasising their heroism and strategic genius. The second is written from the perspective of the defeated, emphasising the suffering of civilians and the brutality of conquest. Both satisfy all three conditions, yet they produce profoundly different understandings of the same events. The definition provides no means of choosing between them or even of acknowledging that the difference matters.
abductive_insight: The background cases reveal that every significant historical narrative is written from a perspective — Thucydides' from within the deliberations of the actors, Gibbon's from a position of ironic detachment, Macaulay's from the Whig tradition — and that the perspective shapes the narrative's explanatory power and coherence. The definition captures the factual and causal dimensions of narrative but omits the perspectival dimension, which determines what counts as significant, how events are weighted, and what understanding the reader is led to form.
passage:
The most influential historical narratives share a feature that goes beyond factual accuracy and causal analysis. Thucydides' account of the Peloponnesian War derived its power from the adoption of the actors' perspective, making their deliberations intelligible to the reader. Gibbon's history of Rome achieved its authority through a perspective of secular irony that revealed patterns invisible to the participants. Macaulay's history of England drew its coherence from a governing Whig perspective that interpreted events as the unfolding of constitutional liberty. In each case, the narrative's success depended not merely on what was told but on the standpoint from which it was told.

One might attempt to characterise a satisfactory narrative as one that recounts events in correct temporal order, is faithful to the documentary evidence, and connects events by identifying causal relations.
