The most durable historical periodisations share a feature that invites reflection. The distinction between the Renaissance and the Baroque captures a transformation that was registered simultaneously across painting, architecture, music, and philosophical thought. The distinction between the age of steam and the age of electricity marks a shift that affected manufacturing, transport, communication, and domestic life alike. The distinction between the Tang and Song dynasties in Chinese history reflects not merely a change of ruling house but a complex restructuring of the examination system, the military, and commercial policy. In each case, the periodisation succeeds because the boundary marks a transformation across multiple dimensions of historical experience.

One might attempt to formalise this by defining an adequate periodisation as one whose boundaries correspond to significant transitions, whose periods are internally coherent, and whose periods exhaust the time-span. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from modern European history. The year 1815 — Napoleon's defeat at Waterloo — marks a significant political transition. The periods it divides exhibit internal coherence with respect to the dominant political arrangements: the Napoleonic order before, the Concert of Europe after. The periods are exhaustive. All three conditions are met. Yet the periodisation is adequate only for one dimension of European life. The industrial transformation, arguably the most consequential development of the era, followed a chronology that pays no regard to 1815. The literary movements of Romanticism and Realism cut across the boundary. One might observe that what the background cases teach — and what the definition fails to require — is that an adequate periodisation capture a transformation registered across multiple dimensions of experience, rather than a change confined to a single domain.

background_cases: When Thucydides narrated the Peloponnesian War, the narrative's power derived not merely from chronological accuracy but from its capacity to make the reader understand how it felt to be caught up in the events — the fear in Athens during the plague, the hubris of the Sicilian expedition, the despair of the Athenian prisoners in the quarries of Syracuse; the narrative rendered the events intelligible by conveying the lived experience of the participants.

When Tacitus narrated the reign of Tiberius, the account succeeded because it conveyed not merely what happened but the psychological atmosphere of the period — the pervasive suspicion, the servility of the senate, the capriciousness of imperial favour; the narrative made the political dynamics intelligible by showing how they were experienced from within.

When Gibbon narrated the fall of the Western Roman Empire, the narrative derived its force from its capacity to convey the grandeur and pathos of the transformation — the reader was made to feel the weight of what was being lost, the slow accumulation of decay, the moments of false hope; the narrative succeeded because it rendered the decline not merely as a sequence of events but as a human experience with emotional and moral dimensions.
definition: An account N constitutes an adequate historical narrative if and only if (i) N presents events in correct chronological order, (ii) N connects events by causal links, and (iii) every factual claim in N is supported by evidence.
conditions: N presents events in correct chronological order
N connects events by causal links
Every factual claim in N is supported by evidence
counterexample: Consider a narrative of the Black Death in Europe that presents every event in correct chronological order — the arrival of the plague in Sicily in 1347, its spread through Italy, France, and England, the demographic collapse, the eventual abatement — connects each event causally to its successor, and supports every claim with documentary evidence. All three conditions are met. Yet suppose the narrative is written in the style of a statistical report, recording dates, mortality figures, and transmission routes without conveying any sense of the terror, the social disintegration, or the religious anguish that characterised the experience of the plague for those who lived through it. The narrative is accurate, causal, and well-evidenced, yet it fails to make the events humanly intelligible.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that the narrative renders events intelligible not merely by establishing causal connections but by conveying the lived experience of the participants — the emotions, the atmosphere, the moral texture of the events as they were undergone. The definition, by requiring only chronological order, causal connection, and evidential support, cannot distinguish a narrative that brings events to life from one that reduces them to a causally connected sequence of facts.
passage:
The greatest historical narratives share a quality that merits examination. When Thucydides recounted the Peloponnesian War, the power of his narrative lay not merely in its chronological accuracy or its causal analysis but in its capacity to convey the lived experience of the participants — the terror of the plague at Athens, the hubris that led to the Sicilian expedition, the agony of the prisoners in the quarries of Syracuse. When Tacitus narrated the reign of Tiberius, the account succeeded because it communicated the psychological atmosphere of the period: the suspicion, the servility, the capriciousness of power. When Gibbon traced the fall of the Western Empire, the narrative's force derived from its evocation of grandeur and pathos — the reader was made to feel the weight of what was passing.

One might attempt to capture this quality by defining an adequate historical narrative as one that presents events in correct chronological order, connects them by causal links, and supports every factual claim with evidence. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from the history of epidemic disease. Consider a narrative of the Black Death that records every event in proper sequence — the arrival in Sicily in 1347, the northward spread, the demographic collapse — connects each event causally to the next, and supports every claim with documentary evidence. All three conditions are met. Yet suppose the narrative reads as a statistical report: dates, mortality percentages, transmission vectors, all presented with clinical detachment. It tells us what happened and why, but it does not make us understand what it was like. One might observe that what the background cases share — and what the definition fails to require — is that the narrative convey the lived experience of the events, rendering them humanly intelligible rather than merely causally explicable.

background_cases: The French Revolution of 1789 is paradigmatically revolutionary not merely because it involved a rapid change of political regime but because the change was animated by a self-conscious ideology — the rights of man, popular sovereignty, the abolition of feudal privilege — that provided the participants with a framework for understanding what they were doing; the revolution was not merely a seizure of power but a transformation guided by a conception of how society ought to be organised.

The English Revolution of the 1640s, despite its complex and contested character, qualifies as revolutionary in part because it was animated by articulate conceptions of parliamentary sovereignty, religious liberty, and the limitations of royal prerogative — ideas that gave the conflict a dimension beyond mere faction and dynastic rivalry.

The American Revolution of 1776 qualifies as revolutionary not merely because it separated the colonies from the crown but because it was guided by a self-conscious political philosophy — the natural rights of individuals, the social contract, the right of resistance to tyranny — that shaped both the conduct of the revolution and the institutions it created.
definition: An event E constitutes a genuine revolution if and only if (i) E involves the overthrow of an existing political order, (ii) E occurs rapidly relative to the normal pace of political change, and (iii) E results in a new political order that is qualitatively different from its predecessor.
conditions: E involves the overthrow of an existing political order
E occurs rapidly relative to the normal pace of political change
E results in a new political order that is qualitatively different from its predecessor
counterexample: Consider a military coup in which a general seizes power, dissolves the parliament, and establishes a military dictatorship within the space of a week. The existing political order is overthrown. The change occurs rapidly. The new order — military dictatorship — is qualitatively different from the parliamentary system it replaced. All three conditions are met. Yet a military coup is not ordinarily regarded as a revolution. It is a seizure of power by an individual or faction, unaccompanied by the ideological framework, the popular mobilisation, or the aspiration to transform social relations that characterise genuine revolutions.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that the revolutionary change is guided by a self-conscious ideology or political philosophy — a set of ideas about how society ought to be organised that animates the participants and shapes the new order they create. The definition, by requiring only overthrow, rapidity, and qualitative difference, cannot distinguish a genuine revolution from a mere coup, which achieves rapid political transformation without the ideological dimension.
passage:
The events most commonly recognised as genuine revolutions share a feature that invites examination. The French Revolution was animated by the ideology of the rights of man and popular sovereignty. The English Revolution, for all its complexity, was shaped by articulate conceptions of parliamentary sovereignty and religious liberty. The American Revolution was guided by a self-conscious philosophy of natural rights and the social contract. In each case, the revolutionary transformation was not merely a seizure of power but a change directed by ideas about how political and social life ought to be organised.

One might attempt to capture this by defining a revolution as the rapid overthrow of an existing political order resulting in a qualitatively new one. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from the history of military coups. Suppose a general seizes power in a single week, dissolves parliament, suspends the constitution, and establishes a military dictatorship. The existing order is overthrown. The change is rapid. The new order — personal rule by a military strongman — is qualitatively different from the parliamentary democracy it replaced. All three conditions are met. Yet one might observe that a coup is not a revolution. It lacks the ideological dimension that characterised the French, English, and American cases. The general has no programme for the transformation of social relations, no articulate conception of political right, no aspiration beyond the consolidation of her own power. What the background cases share — and what the definition fails to require — is that the change be guided by a self-conscious ideology or political philosophy that animates the participants and shapes the new order they create.

background_cases: When Thucydides narrated the Peloponnesian War, the power of his account derived not from the mere temporal ordering of battles and treaties but from the causal connections he drew between the ambitions of Athens, the fears of Sparta, and the resulting diplomatic and military decisions; the narrative illuminated why events unfolded as they did, not merely that they occurred in succession.

When Gibbon narrated the decline and fall of the Roman Empire, the narrative's force lay in the explanatory threads connecting the weakening of civic virtue, the rise of Christianity, and the pressure of barbarian invasions; the temporal sequence alone would not have constituted a narrative in any meaningful sense.

When Macaulay narrated the Glorious Revolution, the account derived its coherence from the causal and thematic connections between the political ambitions of James II, the anxieties of the Protestant establishment, and the intervention of William of Orange; the events were not merely listed but explained.
definition: An account A constitutes a legitimate historical narrative if and only if (i) A recounts events that actually occurred, (ii) the events are presented in their correct chronological order, and (iii) A identifies a beginning, a development, and a conclusion.
conditions: A recounts events that actually occurred
The events are presented in their correct chronological order
A identifies a beginning, a development, and a conclusion
counterexample: Consider an account that begins with the eruption of Vesuvius in 79 AD, proceeds to the coronation of Charlemagne in 800 AD, and concludes with the invention of the printing press in the fifteenth century. Each event actually occurred. They are presented in correct chronological order. The account identifies a beginning, a development, and a conclusion. All three conditions are met. Yet this is not a legitimate historical narrative, for the events are connected by nothing other than temporal succession; there is no causal, thematic, or explanatory thread linking a volcanic eruption, a coronation, and a technological invention.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that the events recounted are connected by causal, thematic, or explanatory relations that make the sequence intelligible as a unified story rather than a mere chronicle. The definition, by requiring only factual accuracy, chronological order, and narrative structure, cannot distinguish genuine narratives from chronological catalogues of unrelated events.
passage:
The historical narratives that have proved most enduring share a structural feature that invites philosophical scrutiny. When Thucydides narrated the Peloponnesian War, the force of his account lay not in the mere temporal ordering of sieges and treaties but in the causal connections he traced between Athenian ambition, Spartan apprehension, and the resulting chain of diplomatic and military decisions. When Gibbon narrated the decline of Rome, his narrative derived its coherence from explanatory threads linking the erosion of civic virtue, the rise of a new religion, and the external pressure of barbarian peoples. When Macaulay treated the Glorious Revolution, the events were rendered intelligible by the connections drawn between the political overreaching of James II, the anxieties of the Protestant establishment, and the calculated intervention of William of Orange.

These cases invite a general characterisation: a legitimate historical narrative is an account of actual events, presented in chronological order, with a beginning, a development, and a conclusion. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be readily constructed. Consider an account that begins with the eruption of Vesuvius in 79 AD, proceeds through the coronation of Charlemagne in 800 AD, and concludes with the invention of the printing press in the fifteenth century. Each event is historical fact. The chronological order is correct. The account possesses a beginning, a development, and a conclusion. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one might observe that this is not a legitimate narrative. The eruption of a volcano, a coronation, and a technological invention are connected by nothing beyond temporal succession. There is no causal thread, no thematic unity, no explanatory relation binding them into a coherent story. What the background cases reveal — and what the definition fails to require — is that the events be connected by relations of cause, theme, or explanation that render the sequence intelligible as something more than an arbitrary chronicle.

background_cases: When historians characterise the abolition of the slave trade as an instance of historical progress, the judgement rests not merely on the observation that later legislation replaced earlier practices but on the recognition that the abolition represented a moral advance — the extension of moral consideration to persons previously excluded — that is defensible by standards independent of the mere passage of time.

When the extension of suffrage to previously disenfranchised groups is cited as progress, the judgement derives its force from the recognition that political participation is a good whose extension represents a genuine improvement in the arrangements of society, not merely a change in those arrangements.

When the development of antiseptic surgical techniques is cited as medical progress, the judgement rests on the recognition that the reduction of surgical mortality represents a genuine improvement in human welfare — a standard that is independent of mere chronological succession.
definition: A transition from one state of affairs S1 to another S2 constitutes historical progress if and only if (i) S2 is later in time than S1, (ii) S2 differs from S1 in respects that the agents of the later period value, and (iii) the transition from S1 to S2 was brought about by deliberate human action.
conditions: S2 is later in time than S1
S2 differs from S1 in respects that the agents of the later period value
The transition from S1 to S2 was brought about by deliberate human action
counterexample: Consider a society that, through deliberate legislative action, replaces a system of progressive taxation with a regime of heavy regressive taxation that enriches a small elite at the expense of the general population. The new arrangement is later in time. It differs from the old in respects that the governing elite — the agents who brought it about — value highly. And the transition was accomplished by deliberate human action. All three conditions are met. Yet we should not regard this as progress; it represents a deterioration in the welfare of the majority, and the fact that the agents responsible for the change value the new arrangement does not suffice to make it progressive.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that the transition is judged progressive not because the agents of the later period happen to value the change, but because the change represents an improvement by standards that are defensible independently of the preferences of those who brought it about — standards of moral consideration, political participation, or human welfare. The definition, by grounding progress in the valuations of the agents of the later period, cannot distinguish genuine progress from changes that are valued by their architects but represent a deterioration by any defensible independent standard.
passage:
Reflection upon the transitions that historians have most confidently identified as instances of progress reveals a common feature. When the abolition of the slave trade is characterised as progressive, the judgement rests on the recognition that the extension of moral consideration to previously excluded persons represents an advance defensible by standards independent of mere temporal succession. When the broadening of suffrage is cited, the force of the judgement derives from the recognition that political participation is a genuine good. When antiseptic surgery is celebrated, the assessment rests on the plain fact that reducing surgical mortality represents an improvement in human welfare.

One might attempt to capture this by defining progress as a transition in which the later state differs from the earlier in respects valued by the agents of the later period, where the change was brought about by deliberate action. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from fiscal policy. Suppose a society, through deliberate legislation, replaces a progressive tax system with a heavily regressive regime that concentrates wealth in the hands of a small governing elite. The new arrangement is later in time. It differs from the old in respects that the elite — the agents who engineered the change — value greatly: their personal wealth has increased, their political power has been consolidated. The transition was plainly deliberate. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Yet one might observe that this is not progress. The welfare of the majority has deteriorated. The change is valued only by those who benefit from it. What the background cases reveal — and what the definition fails to require — is that the improvement be assessable by standards independent of the preferences of those who brought the change about: standards of moral consideration, equitable distribution, or general human welfare.

background_cases: When historians classify ancient Egypt as a civilisation, the judgement rests not merely on the presence of monumental architecture and administrative complexity but on the recognition that these achievements were sustained over millennia through institutions capable of transmitting accumulated practices, techniques, and organisational forms from one generation to the next.

When Mesopotamia is classified as a civilisation, the classification reflects not merely the development of writing and urban life but the capacity of Mesopotamian institutions — temples, scribal schools, legal codes — to maintain continuity and accumulate innovations across centuries of political upheaval.

When classical China is recognised as a civilisation, the judgement acknowledges that the examination system, the bureaucratic apparatus, and the literary tradition constituted mechanisms of intergenerational transmission that sustained a recognisable cultural identity across successive dynasties.
definition: A society S constitutes a civilisation if and only if (i) S has achieved a high degree of social complexity, including urban settlements and occupational specialisation, (ii) S has produced significant cultural achievements in art, architecture, or learning, and (iii) S encompasses a large population distributed over a substantial geographical area.
conditions: S has achieved a high degree of social complexity, including urban settlements and occupational specialisation
S has produced significant cultural achievements in art, architecture, or learning
S encompasses a large population distributed over a substantial geographical area
counterexample: Consider a vast nomadic confederation that, at its peak, encompasses millions of people spread across an enormous territory, maintains a highly complex social hierarchy with specialised military, administrative, and artisanal roles, and produces remarkable achievements in metalwork, textile arts, and oral poetry. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet suppose the confederation dissolves within a single generation upon the death of its founder, leaving no enduring institutions, no written records, and no mechanism for transmitting its organisational innovations to successors. We should hesitate to call this a civilisation in the fullest sense, for its achievements were not sustained by institutions capable of intergenerational transmission.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that the societies recognised as civilisations possess institutions capable of sustaining their achievements across generations — mechanisms of transmission that ensure continuity beyond the lifespan of any individual or ruling group. The definition, by requiring only complexity, cultural achievement, and scale at a single moment, cannot distinguish civilisations from transient confederations that achieve impressive scale and complexity but lack the institutional means to endure.
passage:
The societies that historians have most confidently classified as civilisations share a feature that invites careful analysis. When ancient Egypt is recognised as a civilisation, the classification reflects not merely the grandeur of its pyramids or the intricacy of its administration but the capacity of Egyptian institutions — the priesthood, the scribal tradition, the bureaucratic apparatus — to sustain these achievements across millennia. When Mesopotamia is similarly classified, the judgement acknowledges the role of temples, scribal schools, and legal codes in maintaining continuity through centuries of political turbulence. When classical China is recognised, the examination system and the literary tradition are understood as mechanisms of intergenerational transmission that preserved a recognisable cultural identity across successive dynasties.

One might attempt to capture this by defining a civilisation as a society that has achieved social complexity with urban settlements and specialised occupations, has produced significant cultural achievements, and encompasses a large population over a substantial area. Consider a vast nomadic confederation that, at its zenith, encompasses millions of people across an enormous territory. It maintains a complex social hierarchy with specialised military, administrative, and artisanal roles. It produces remarkable achievements in metalwork, textile arts, and oral epic poetry. All three conditions of the proposed definition are met. Yet suppose the confederation dissolves entirely within a generation of its founder's death, leaving no enduring institutions, no written records, and no means of transmitting its organisational or cultural achievements to future peoples. One might observe that what the background cases reveal — and what the definition fails to require — is that a civilisation must possess institutions capable of sustaining its achievements across generations, ensuring continuity beyond the span of any individual lifetime or political arrangement.

background_cases: When the French Revolution of 1789 is characterised as a genuine revolution, the judgement reflects not merely the overthrow of the monarchy but the transformation of the fundamental principles upon which political authority was understood to rest — the replacement of divine right and hereditary privilege with popular sovereignty and civic equality as the legitimating basis of government.

When the English Revolution of the 1640s is so characterised, the classification reflects the transformation of the principle of political legitimacy from the divine right of kings to parliamentary authority — a change in the conceptual foundations of governance, not merely in its personnel.

When the American Revolution is cited, the judgement reflects the replacement of colonial subjection with the principle of self-government grounded in the consent of the governed — a fundamental reconception of the basis of political authority.
definition: An event E constitutes a genuine political revolution if and only if (i) E results in a change of government, (ii) the new government differs from the old in its institutional structure, and (iii) the change is accomplished rapidly rather than through gradual evolution.
conditions: E results in a change of government
The new government differs from the old in its institutional structure
The change is accomplished rapidly rather than through gradual evolution
counterexample: Consider a rapid change of government in which a hereditary monarchy is replaced, following a swift popular uprising, by an oligarchy of wealthy landowners who institute a different set of governing bodies — a council, a treasury, a judiciary — all structurally distinct from the old royal court. The change is rapid. The government changes. The institutional structure is different. All three conditions are met. Yet suppose the fundamental principle of political authority remains unchanged: government is held to be the prerogative of those who possess wealth and social standing, just as it was the prerogative of those who possessed royal blood. The basis of legitimacy has not been reconceived; it has merely been transferred from one privileged class to another.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that the revolution involved a transformation of the fundamental principles upon which political authority is understood to rest — not merely a change in institutional structure or governing personnel, but a reconception of the very basis of political legitimacy. The definition, by requiring only a rapid change of government and institutional structure, cannot distinguish genuine revolutions from rapid transfers of power that leave the underlying principles of legitimacy unaltered.
passage:
The political upheavals that historians have most confidently classified as genuine revolutions share a feature that transcends mere institutional change. When the French Revolution is so characterised, the judgement reflects not merely the replacement of a monarchy with a republic but the transformation of the principles upon which political authority was understood to rest: divine right and hereditary privilege gave way to popular sovereignty and civic equality. When the English Revolution of the 1640s is classified as a revolution, the designation acknowledges that parliamentary authority replaced the divine right of kings as the legitimating basis of government. When the American Revolution is cited, the force of the classification derives from the replacement of colonial subjection with the principle of self-government grounded in consent.

One might propose that a genuine revolution is a rapid change of government resulting in a new institutional structure. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be constructed from the history of oligarchic coups. Suppose a hereditary monarchy is swiftly overthrown by a popular uprising, and in its place a council of wealthy landowners establishes a new government with entirely different institutions — a deliberative council, an independent treasury, a reorganised judiciary. The change is rapid. The government is new. The institutional structure differs markedly from the old regime. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet suppose the fundamental principle of political authority has not changed: under the monarchy, government was the prerogative of those who possessed noble blood; under the new oligarchy, it is the prerogative of those who possess landed wealth. The basis of legitimacy has merely been transferred from one privileged class to another. One might observe that what the background cases reveal — and what the definition fails to capture — is that a genuine revolution transforms the very principles upon which political authority rests, not merely the institutions through which it is exercised.

background_cases: When Thucydides explained the outbreak of the Peloponnesian War, he distinguished between the immediate pretexts — the disputes over Corcyra and Potidaea — and the underlying cause, which he identified as the growth of Athenian power and the fear this inspired in Sparta. The explanation derived its force from the identification of a structural condition whose operative role could be traced through the particular decisions and events that led to hostilities.

When historians explain the fall of the Western Roman Empire, the most enduring accounts identify not merely a catalogue of contributing factors — barbarian invasions, economic decline, political instability — but the interaction among these factors: the way in which economic contraction undermined the capacity to maintain frontier defences, which in turn invited further invasions, producing a self-reinforcing cycle of decline.

When Tocqueville explained the French Revolution, he argued that the Revolution's causes lay not in the sudden eruption of grievances but in the long-term centralisation of administrative power under the ancien régime, which had simultaneously raised expectations and destroyed the intermediate institutions through which grievances might have been peacefully addressed. The explanation derived its power from tracing a structural transformation over decades that made the eventual upheaval intelligible.
definition: An account A provides a satisfactory historical explanation of an event E if and only if (i) A cites causes that preceded E in time, (ii) A draws upon evidence from primary sources contemporary with E, and (iii) A places E within a broader narrative that connects it to other events in the same period.
conditions: A cites causes that preceded E in time
A draws upon evidence from primary sources contemporary with E
A places E within a broader narrative that connects it to other events in the same period
counterexample: Suppose a historian explains the outbreak of a peasant uprising in eighteenth-century France by citing the preceding causes of poor harvests and oppressive taxation, drawing upon contemporary tax records and parish registers, and placing the uprising within a broader narrative of rural discontent that connects it to similar disturbances in neighbouring provinces. All three conditions are met. Yet the account merely lists contributing factors and locates the event within a chronological sequence, without identifying the mechanism by which poor harvests and taxation produced revolt in this particular province at this particular time, while the same conditions persisted for decades in other provinces without provoking any uprising.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that the explanation identifies not merely a catalogue of antecedent conditions but a structural mechanism or dynamic process through which those conditions produced the specific event — the way in which the factors interacted to make the outcome not merely possible but, given the particular configuration of circumstances, intelligible. The definition fails to require the identification of such a mechanism, permitting catalogues of antecedent conditions to pass as explanations.

background_cases: When historians divide the history of Western art into Renaissance, Baroque, and Neoclassical periods, the periodisation captures a genuine sequence of stylistic transformations: the shift from the geometric perspective and humanistic idealism of the Renaissance to the dramatic chiaroscuro and emotional intensity of the Baroque, and thence to the restrained classicism of the Neoclassical. The boundaries correspond to changes that were recognised by contemporaries, not merely imposed retrospectively.

When economic historians periodise the Industrial Revolution into phases of textile mechanisation, railway expansion, and electrification, the divisions reflect changes that were experienced as transformative by the populations who lived through them — changes in the nature of work, in patterns of settlement, and in the rhythms of daily life.

When historians of science divide the seventeenth century into pre-Newtonian and post-Newtonian phases, the periodisation captures a genuine intellectual transformation: the replacement of a qualitative, Aristotelian framework with a quantitative, mathematical one that fundamentally altered the conduct and aims of natural philosophy.
definition: A periodisation of a domain of history is adequate if and only if (i) each period is characterised by internally coherent features that distinguish it from adjacent periods, (ii) the transitions between periods are marked by identifiable turning points, and (iii) the periodisation is accepted by a consensus of scholars working in the domain.
conditions: Each period is characterised by internally coherent features that distinguish it from adjacent periods
The transitions between periods are marked by identifiable turning points
The periodisation is accepted by a consensus of scholars working in the domain
counterexample: Consider a periodisation of Chinese history that divides it according to the succession of ruling dynasties: Tang, Song, Yuan, Ming, Qing. Each dynastic period is internally coherent in terms of its governing institutions. The transitions are marked by identifiable turning points — the fall of one dynasty and the rise of another. And this periodisation enjoys the consensus of a large body of scholars. All three conditions are met. Yet the periodisation may obscure the fact that some of the most profound cultural and economic transformations in Chinese history — such as the commercialisation of agriculture or the spread of printing — did not coincide with dynastic transitions at all, and that certain dynastic boundaries mark little more than a change of ruling house with minimal alteration in the structures of daily life.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that the periodisation captures transformations in the substantive features of the domain — stylistic, economic, or intellectual changes experienced or recognisable as such by those who lived through them — rather than merely tracking changes in a single institutional variable such as political succession. The definition fails to require that the characterising features of the periods be substantively relevant to the domain being periodised, rather than merely formally coherent and consensually accepted.

The most illuminating historical periodisations share a feature that the standard account of adequate periodisation fails to require. Suppose one divides Chinese history according to the succession of ruling dynasties: Tang, Song, Yuan, Ming, Qing. One might observe that what the background cases share — and what this definition fails to require — is that the periods capture substantive transformations in the domain being periodised, rather than merely tracking a single institutional variable whose changes may be formally crisp but substantively superficial.

background_cases: When Gibbon narrated the decline and fall of the Roman Empire, the power of his account derived not merely from the accuracy of his facts and the elegance of his prose but from the selection and arrangement of events in a manner that rendered the process of decline intelligible — each event was presented not merely as having occurred but as bearing a specific relation to the forces driving the empire's disintegration.

When Macaulay narrated the Glorious Revolution, the narrative's force lay in its capacity to present the constitutional settlement of 1688 as the culmination of a long struggle between parliamentary prerogative and royal absolutism. The arrangement of events was not arbitrary; it was governed by a thesis about the meaning and direction of the process being narrated.

When Braudel narrated the history of the Mediterranean world, the narrative's distinctive power derived from its organisation around the longue durée — the slow structural transformations of geography, economy, and climate that shaped the possibilities within which particular events occurred. The narrative was governed by a conception of historical temporality that determined which events were selected and how they were connected.
definition: A historical narrative N is adequate if and only if (i) every factual claim in N is supported by evidence, (ii) N is free from logical contradictions, and (iii) N presents events in a temporal sequence that reflects their actual chronological order.
conditions: Every factual claim in N is supported by evidence
N is free from logical contradictions
N presents events in a temporal sequence that reflects their actual chronological order
counterexample: Suppose a historian constructs a narrative of the English Civil War that faithfully records every major battle, parliamentary debate, and royal decree in correct chronological order, with each claim supported by primary evidence and no logical contradiction anywhere in the account. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the narrative is a bare chronicle — a sequence of dated facts with no interpretive framework, no thesis about why the war occurred or what its outcome signified, and no principle of selection that would distinguish the causally important events from the incidental. One event follows another without any indication of how it contributed to or resulted from the processes at work.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that the narrative is governed by an interpretive framework or thesis that determines the selection, emphasis, and arrangement of events in a manner that renders the historical process intelligible — that gives the sequence a meaning beyond mere temporal succession. The definition fails to require that N be organised by such a framework, thereby permitting bare chronicles to qualify as adequate narratives.

These cases invite a general characterisation: an adequate historical narrative is one in which every factual claim is supported by evidence, the account is free from logical contradictions, and the events are presented in correct chronological order. One might observe that what the background cases share — and what this definition fails to require — is an interpretive framework governing the selection, emphasis, and arrangement of events in a manner that renders the historical process intelligible, giving the sequence a meaning that transcends mere temporal succession.

background_cases: When historians characterise the French Revolution as a genuine revolution, they point not merely to the overthrow of the monarchy and the declaration of a republic but to the fundamental reconstitution of the social order: the abolition of feudal privileges, the redistribution of land, the transformation of legal and educational institutions, and the emergence of new forms of political participation that persisted long after the revolutionary period itself.

When historians characterise the English Revolution of the seventeenth century, they emphasise not merely the execution of the king and the establishment of the Commonwealth but the lasting changes in the relationship between Crown and Parliament — changes that were consolidated rather than reversed by the Restoration and that fundamentally altered the constitutional structure of the state.

When historians characterise the Industrial Revolution as a revolution, they point to the transformation of the entire mode of production: the replacement of manual labour by machine manufacture, the shift from rural to urban settlement, and the reconstitution of social relations around the factory and the wage. The revolution was not an event but a structural transformation whose effects were irreversible.
definition: An upheaval U constitutes a genuine historical revolution if and only if (i) U overthrows an existing political regime, (ii) U involves widespread violence or mass mobilisation, and (iii) U establishes a new regime whose formal constitution differs fundamentally from that of its predecessor.
conditions: U overthrows an existing political regime
U involves widespread violence or mass mobilisation
U establishes a new regime whose formal constitution differs fundamentally from that of its predecessor
counterexample: Consider a military coup in which generals overthrow a civilian government, mobilise large numbers of troops, and establish a new regime with a fundamentally different formal constitution — replacing a parliamentary democracy with a one-party state whose constitution vests all power in a supreme military council. All three conditions are met. Yet suppose that within a decade the same economic elites control the same industries, the same landowners dominate the same estates, the same bureaucrats administer the same policies, and the daily life of the population is essentially unchanged. The formal political transformation masks a profound continuity in the actual structure of social and economic power.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that the revolution produced lasting changes not merely in the formal political constitution but in the deep social, economic, and institutional structures of the society — changes that were irreversible and that reconstituted the fundamental relations of power and daily life. The definition fails to require that U produce such structural transformation, permitting merely formal or superficial changes of regime to qualify as revolutions.
