The most instructive cases of historical revolution share a structural feature that the most straightforward definitions fail to require. When historians characterise the French Revolution as genuine, they point not merely to the overthrow of the monarchy but to the fundamental reconstitution of the social order: the abolition of feudal privileges, the redistribution of land, the transformation of legal institutions, and the emergence of new forms of political participation that persisted long after the revolutionary period. When the English Revolution is characterised as such, historians emphasise the lasting alteration in the relationship between Crown and Parliament — changes consolidated rather than reversed by the Restoration. When the Industrial Revolution is called a revolution, the term denotes a structural transformation of the entire mode of production whose effects were irreversible.

These cases invite a general characterisation: a genuine revolution is one that overthrows an existing regime, involves widespread violence or mass mobilisation, and establishes a new regime whose formal constitution differs fundamentally from its predecessor. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be constructed from the history of military coups. Suppose a group of generals overthrows a civilian government, mobilises large numbers of troops, and establishes a one-party state whose constitution vests all power in a supreme military council — a formal constitution fundamentally different from the parliamentary democracy it replaced. All three conditions of the proposed definition are met. Yet suppose that within a decade the same economic elites control the same industries, the same landowners dominate the same estates, the same bureaucrats administer the same policies under new titles, and the daily life of the population is essentially unchanged. The formal political transformation masks a profound continuity in the actual structures of social and economic power. One might observe that what the background cases share — and what the definition fails to require — is that the revolution produce lasting changes in the deep social, economic, and institutional structures of the society, not merely in the formal character of its political constitution.

background_cases: When the abolition of the slave trade was achieved in the British Empire in the early nineteenth century, the event was widely regarded as an instance of moral progress: a practice that had been accepted for centuries came to be recognised as fundamentally unjust, and legislation was enacted to prohibit it.

When the franchise was extended to women in several Western nations in the early twentieth century, the extension was understood as progress in political morality: a group that had been excluded from the fundamental political right of self-governance was admitted to participation on equal terms.

When the Geneva Conventions codified the treatment of prisoners of war and civilians in armed conflict, the codification was taken as evidence of progress in international morality: norms that had previously been observed only sporadically and informally were given institutional expression and binding legal force.
definition: Historical moral progress has occurred when (i) a previously accepted practice is recognised as unjust and abandoned, (ii) the recognition of injustice is embodied in lasting institutional or legal reform, and (iii) the reformed state of affairs is more just than the prior state by the standards of the reformers themselves.
conditions: A previously accepted practice is recognised as unjust and abandoned
The recognition of injustice is embodied in lasting institutional or legal reform
The reformed state of affairs is more just than the prior state by the standards of the reformers themselves
counterexample: Suppose a society in the eighteenth century abolishes the practice of public judicial torture and replaces it with imprisonment. The practice of torture is recognised as unjust and abandoned. The abolition is embodied in lasting legal reform. And the reformed state of affairs is regarded by the reformers as more just. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the prisons that replace torture become sites of prolonged psychological suffering, disease, and degradation, producing forms of misery that the reformers did not anticipate and that later generations come to regard as themselves unjust. The progress is real from the standpoint of the reformers but illusory from the standpoint of those who suffer under the new institutions.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that the moral progress they represent was assessed from a particular temporal standpoint. The definition, by judging progress solely by the standards of the reformers themselves, fails to require that the reformed state of affairs be evaluated against the full range of its actual consequences, including consequences unforeseen by the reformers. Genuine moral progress, it appears, requires not merely the intention to achieve greater justice but the actual achievement of it, as assessed by standards that may transcend those available to the reformers at the time.
passage:
Reflection upon the most widely acknowledged instances of moral progress in the modern era reveals a structural pattern. When the British Empire abolished the slave trade, when the franchise was extended to women, and when the Geneva Conventions codified the protection of prisoners and civilians, each case involved the recognition of a previously accepted practice as unjust, the embodiment of that recognition in lasting institutional reform, and the achievement of a state of affairs judged more just by the standards of the reformers.

These cases invite a general characterisation: moral progress occurs when an unjust practice is recognised, abandoned, and replaced by lasting reform that is more just by the reformers' own standards. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from the history of penal reform. Suppose a society in the eighteenth century abolishes public judicial torture and replaces it with incarceration. Torture is recognised as unjust and abandoned. The abolition is embodied in lasting legal reform. The reformers regard imprisonment as a more humane and just response to crime. All three conditions are met. Yet the prisons that replace torture become sites of prolonged psychological suffering, disease, and degradation, producing forms of misery that the reformers neither anticipated nor intended, and that later generations come to regard as themselves deeply unjust. The progress is real from the standpoint of the reformers but doubtful from the standpoint of those who actually endure the consequences. What the background cases reveal — and what the definition fails to require — is that genuine moral progress must be assessed not only by the intentions of the reformers but by the actual consequences of the reform, evaluated against standards that may transcend those available at the time of the reform itself.

background_cases: When Thucydides narrated the Peloponnesian War, the narrative's authority derived in part from its restriction to events that Thucydides himself had witnessed or could verify through the testimony of participants. The narrative was grounded in identifiable sources whose reliability could be assessed.

When Gibbon composed the Decline and Fall of the Roman Empire, the narrative's scholarly character rested upon its systematic citation of ancient sources and its critical evaluation of their reliability. The reader was able to trace the evidential basis of each major claim.

When Ranke established the seminar method and insisted that history be written 'as it actually happened,' the principle rested upon the requirement that the historian consult original documents and subject them to critical scrutiny, so that the narrative was answerable to the surviving evidence.
definition: A historical narrative N is methodologically sound if and only if (i) every factual claim in N is supported by identifiable primary sources, (ii) N subjects its sources to critical evaluation for reliability and bias, and (iii) N does not assert any claim that contradicts the available evidence.
conditions: Every factual claim in N is supported by identifiable primary sources
N subjects its sources to critical evaluation for reliability and bias
N does not assert any claim that contradicts the available evidence
counterexample: Suppose a historian writes a narrative of a colonial war that satisfies all three conditions: every claim is supported by identifiable primary sources, the sources are critically evaluated, and no claim contradicts the evidence. Yet the only surviving primary sources are the administrative records and military dispatches of the colonial power; the colonised population left no written records. The narrative, though methodologically impeccable by the stated criteria, tells the story entirely from the perspective of one side and cannot represent the experiences, motivations, or suffering of the other.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share — and what the definition fails to capture — is that the historians in question worked within evidentiary environments where the relevant perspectives were recoverable from the surviving sources. The definition, by requiring only that claims be supported by identifiable primary sources critically evaluated, does not address the possibility that the surviving sources may systematically exclude the perspectives of significant historical actors, so that a narrative satisfying every stated condition may nevertheless be profoundly one-sided.
passage:
The most celebrated practitioners of historical method share a commitment to grounding narrative in identifiable, critically evaluated sources. When Thucydides restricted his account to events he had witnessed or could verify through participant testimony, when Gibbon systematically cited and evaluated ancient authorities, and when Ranke insisted upon consultation of original documents, each was articulating a principle of evidential accountability that has come to be regarded as foundational to the discipline.

These cases invite a general characterisation: a methodologically sound narrative supports every factual claim with identifiable primary sources, subjects those sources to critical evaluation, and asserts nothing that contradicts the evidence. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from the historiography of colonial wars. Suppose a historian composes a narrative of a conflict between a colonial power and a colonised population. Every factual claim is supported by identifiable primary sources — administrative records, military dispatches, diplomatic correspondence — all of which have been critically evaluated for reliability and bias. No claim contradicts the available evidence. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the only surviving primary sources are those produced by the colonial administration; the colonised population, lacking access to print or having had its records destroyed, left no written documents. The narrative, though methodologically impeccable by the stated criteria, tells the story entirely from one side. What the background cases reveal — and what the definition fails to require — is that methodological soundness demands not only fidelity to the sources that survive but an awareness of the sources that do not, and a recognition that the evidential record may systematically exclude the perspectives of significant historical actors.

background_cases: When historians designated the fall of the Western Roman Empire in 476 as the boundary between antiquity and the medieval period, the periodisation rested upon the recognition of a dramatic political discontinuity: the cessation of imperial authority in the West. This single event was taken to mark a transformation sufficiently profound to warrant a new temporal category.

When the Renaissance was identified as a distinct period beginning in fourteenth-century Italy, the periodisation rested upon the recognition of a cluster of cultural transformations — the revival of classical learning, new artistic techniques, and the humanist reorientation of intellectual life — that collectively distinguished the period from what preceded it.

When the Industrial Revolution was identified as a distinct period beginning in late eighteenth-century Britain, the periodisation rested upon the recognition of a cluster of economic and technological transformations — the mechanisation of production, the factory system, and the shift from agrarian to industrial economy — that collectively altered the character of social life.
definition: A period P is a legitimate division of historical time if and only if (i) P is bounded by events or transformations that mark a qualitative change in the character of social, political, or cultural life, (ii) the features that characterise P are distinctive relative to the periods that precede and follow it, and (iii) the periodisation is recognised as useful by a community of practising historians.
conditions: P is bounded by events or transformations that mark a qualitative change in social, political, or cultural life
The features that characterise P are distinctive relative to the periods that precede and follow it
The periodisation is recognised as useful by a community of practising historians
counterexample: Suppose a community of European historians recognises a periodisation that divides African history into a pre-colonial period, a colonial period, and a post-colonial period. The boundaries correspond to transformations that mark qualitative changes in African political life. The features of each period are distinctive. And the periodisation is recognised as useful by the community. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the periodisation imposes a European-centred framework upon African history, defining its temporal structure entirely by reference to the arrival and departure of European colonial powers. The internal dynamics of African societies — their own political, cultural, and economic rhythms — are subordinated to a periodisation that privileges the European experience as the organising principle.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that the periodisations in question were generated from within the historical experience of the civilisations being periodised: the fall of Rome was a discontinuity experienced by the inhabitants of the empire, the Renaissance was a cultural movement recognised by its participants, and the Industrial Revolution was an economic transformation lived through by the affected populations. The definition, by requiring only qualitative change, distinctiveness, and professional usefulness, fails to require that the periodisation reflect the temporal structure of the society being periodised rather than the temporal structure imposed by an external perspective.
passage:
The most widely accepted periodisations in historiography share a feature that the most natural definitions of legitimate periodisation fail to require. When the fall of the Western Roman Empire was taken to mark the boundary between antiquity and the medieval period, the periodisation reflected a discontinuity experienced within the civilisation itself. When the Renaissance was identified as a distinct period in Italian history, the identification rested upon cultural transformations recognised and celebrated by contemporaries. When the Industrial Revolution was designated a period in British history, the designation reflected economic changes lived through by the affected population.

These cases invite a general characterisation: a legitimate period is bounded by qualitative transformations, characterised by distinctive features, and recognised as useful by practising historians. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from the periodisation of African history. Suppose a community of European historians divides African history into pre-colonial, colonial, and post-colonial periods. The boundaries correspond to transformations that mark qualitative changes in African political life. The features of each period are distinctive. The periodisation is recognised as useful by the scholarly community. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the periodisation defines the temporal structure of African history entirely by reference to the arrival and departure of European colonial powers. The internal dynamics of African societies — their own political cycles, cultural movements, and economic transformations — are subordinated to a framework that privileges the European experience as the organising principle. What the background cases reveal is that legitimate periodisation must reflect the temporal structure of the society being periodised, not one imposed from an external vantage point.

background_cases: When the legacy of the Roman legal system was assessed, its significance was measured by the extent to which Roman legal concepts — the distinction between public and private law, the codification of contractual obligations, the principle of legal personality — continued to structure the legal systems of later civilisations, providing a framework that persisted long after the empire itself had dissolved.

When the legacy of the Greek philosophical tradition was assessed, its significance rested upon the continuing influence of Greek conceptual innovations — the distinction between form and matter, the analysis of logical validity, the concept of natural law — upon subsequent intellectual traditions, even when those traditions rejected specific Greek conclusions.

When the legacy of the Confucian tradition was assessed in the context of East Asian civilisation, its significance lay in the persistence of Confucian concepts of filial piety, meritocratic governance, and ritual propriety as organising principles of social and political life across centuries and across multiple societies.
definition: A historical institution, tradition, or body of thought L has left a significant legacy if and only if (i) L produced ideas, practices, or structures that were transmitted to subsequent generations, (ii) those transmitted elements exercised a demonstrable influence upon the development of later institutions, traditions, or bodies of thought, and (iii) the influence of L is acknowledged by the later communities that were affected by it.
conditions: L produced ideas, practices, or structures that were transmitted to subsequent generations
Those transmitted elements exercised a demonstrable influence upon the development of later institutions or traditions
The influence of L is acknowledged by the later communities that were affected by it
counterexample: Consider the legacy of Arabic mathematical and scientific scholarship in medieval Europe. Arab scholars preserved, translated, and extended Greek mathematical and scientific works, and transmitted them to medieval Europe through translations into Latin. The transmitted elements — algebra, the Hindu-Arabic numeral system, advances in optics and medicine — exercised a demonstrable and profound influence upon the development of European science. The first two conditions are clearly satisfied. Yet for centuries, European scholars either did not acknowledge or actively obscured the Arabic origins of these contributions, attributing the ideas to ancient Greek sources or to European innovation. The third condition is not satisfied, yet the legacy is undeniably significant.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that the legacies in question were recognised by the receiving traditions. But the case of Arabic scholarship reveals that a legacy may be profoundly significant even when the communities it influenced fail or refuse to acknowledge its origin. The definition, by requiring acknowledgement, conflates the significance of a legacy with the willingness of later communities to recognise it, thereby excluding cases of unacknowledged or suppressed influence that are among the most historically consequential.
passage:
The most instructive cases of historical legacy share a common structure: ideas, practices, or institutions produced by one tradition are transmitted to subsequent generations and demonstrably shape the development of later traditions. The Roman legal system bequeathed concepts that continued to structure law for centuries. The Greek philosophical tradition transmitted innovations that shaped subsequent intellectual life even when its specific conclusions were rejected. The Confucian tradition provided organising principles that persisted across centuries and multiple societies.

These cases invite a general characterisation: a significant legacy is one whose transmitted elements demonstrably influence later developments and whose influence is acknowledged by the affected communities. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from the transmission of Arabic scholarship to medieval Europe. Arab scholars preserved, translated, and extended Greek mathematical and scientific works, contributing original advances in algebra, optics, and medicine, and transmitting this body of learning to Europe through Latin translations. The influence upon the development of European science was demonstrable and profound. Yet for centuries, European scholars either failed to acknowledge or actively suppressed the Arabic origins of these contributions, attributing the ideas instead to ancient Greek or European sources. The third condition of the definition is not satisfied, yet the legacy is undeniably among the most significant in intellectual history. What the background cases reveal — and what the definition fails to recognise — is that the significance of a legacy is independent of whether the communities it influenced choose to acknowledge it, and that a definition requiring such acknowledgement excludes some of the most consequential cases of cultural transmission.

background_cases: When the abolition of the slave trade in the British Empire was accomplished in 1807 and the full emancipation of enslaved persons in 1833, these events were widely regarded as instances of moral progress: a practice that had been accepted for centuries was recognised as unjust and legally terminated.

When the expansion of the franchise to include women in the early twentieth century was achieved in many Western nations, the reform was regarded as a clear instance of progress: a class of persons previously excluded from political participation was granted the rights of citizenship.

When the eradication of smallpox was confirmed by the World Health Organisation in 1980, the achievement was regarded as progress in the most unambiguous sense: a disease that had killed millions was permanently eliminated through coordinated human effort.
definition: An historical change C constitutes genuine progress if and only if (i) C remedies a condition that was widely regarded as unjust, harmful, or deficient before C occurred, (ii) C is brought about through deliberate human effort rather than by accident, and (iii) the benefits of C are durable and not easily reversed.
conditions: C remedies a condition widely regarded as unjust harmful or deficient before C occurred
C is brought about through deliberate human effort
The benefits of C are durable and not easily reversed
counterexample: Suppose a colonial administration in the nineteenth century constructs a railway network across a territory, connecting previously isolated communities and enabling trade. The railway remedies a condition — isolation and economic stagnation — that was widely regarded as deficient. It is brought about through deliberate human effort and planning. And the infrastructure proves durable, remaining in use for over a century. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the railway was built using forced labour drawn from the subjugated indigenous population, and its primary purpose was to extract natural resources for the benefit of the colonial power. The progress in connectivity and trade was purchased at the cost of exploitation and dispossession that the definition does not require one to consider.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share — and what the definition fails to capture — is that genuine progress requires not only the remedying of a deficient condition but also the justice of the means by which the remedy is achieved. The abolition of slavery, the extension of the franchise, and the eradication of smallpox were accomplished by means that were themselves broadly consistent with the moral principles that the reforms embodied. A definition that attends only to the end state and its durability, without inquiring into the justice of the process, admits as progressive changes that are achieved through means that are themselves unjust.
passage:
The most widely acknowledged instances of historical progress share a structural feature that invites generalisation. The abolition of the slave trade remedied a condition universally recognised as unjust, through deliberate political effort, and the achievement proved durable. The extension of the franchise to women corrected an exclusion that reformers had long denounced, through sustained campaigning, and the reform was not reversed. The eradication of smallpox eliminated a scourge that had killed millions, through coordinated international effort, and the result has endured.

These cases suggest a general characterisation: genuine progress consists in the deliberate remedying of a condition widely regarded as unjust or deficient, producing benefits that are durable. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from the history of colonial infrastructure. Suppose a colonial administration constructs a railway network across a territory, connecting previously isolated communities and enabling trade. The railway remedies a condition widely regarded as deficient — isolation and economic stagnation — through deliberate effort, and the infrastructure proves durable, remaining in use for more than a century. All three conditions are met. Yet the railway was built using forced labour drawn from the subjugated indigenous population, and its primary purpose was the extraction of natural resources for the colonial power. What the background cases reveal is that progress requires not merely the remedying of a deficient condition but also the justice of the means by which the remedy is achieved. The abolition of slavery, the extension of the franchise, and the eradication of disease were accomplished through means broadly consistent with the principles they embodied. A definition that attends only to outcomes and durability, without examining the process, admits as progressive changes wrought by exploitation.

background_cases: When Thucydides narrated the Peloponnesian War, the credibility of his account rested in large part upon his having been a participant in the events he described. His proximity to the events gave his narrative an authority that no later reconstruction could possess.

When Bede narrated the history of the English church, the credibility of his account rested upon his careful citation of documentary sources, including letters, charters, and the testimony of witnesses whom he named and whose reliability he assessed.

When Ranke insisted that the historian's task was to show how things actually were, he grounded historical credibility in the critical examination of primary sources, distinguishing first-hand reports from later compilations and evaluating each source for bias and reliability.
definition: A historical narrative N is credible if and only if (i) N draws upon sources that are proximate to the events narrated, (ii) N subjects those sources to critical evaluation for bias and reliability, and (iii) N presents a coherent account in which the events are connected by plausible causal links.
conditions: N draws upon sources proximate to the events narrated
N subjects sources to critical evaluation for bias and reliability
N presents a coherent account with plausible causal links
counterexample: Suppose a historian writes a narrative of a peasant uprising in medieval Europe. She draws upon sources proximate to the events: court records, manorial rolls, and royal correspondence. She subjects each source to careful critical evaluation, identifying the biases of each author and assessing their reliability. And she presents a coherent account in which the events are connected by plausible causal links. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet every source she employs was produced by the literate landowning class, and the voices of the peasants themselves are entirely absent. The narrative is coherent, critically grounded, and based upon proximate sources, yet it tells the story exclusively from the perspective of those against whom the uprising was directed.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share — and what the definition fails to capture — is that the credibility of a historical narrative depends not only upon the proximity, critical evaluation, and causal coherence of the sources employed, but upon the representativeness of those sources with respect to the perspectives of all significant parties to the events. A narrative constructed from critically evaluated proximate sources may still be profoundly one-sided if the surviving sources represent only one party to the events narrated.
passage:
The most influential reflections upon historical method converge upon a common set of requirements. When Thucydides narrated the Peloponnesian War, his credibility rested upon proximity to the events described. When Bede wrote his ecclesiastical history, he cited documents and named witnesses. When Ranke articulated the ideal of showing how things actually were, he grounded credibility in the critical examination of primary sources.

These cases invite a general characterisation: a historical narrative is credible when it draws upon proximate sources, subjects them to critical evaluation, and presents a coherent account with plausible causal connections. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from the study of medieval social conflict. Suppose a historian writes a narrative of a peasant uprising, drawing upon court records, manorial rolls, and royal correspondence — all proximate to the events. She evaluates each source critically, identifying the biases of the authors and assessing their reliability. And she constructs a coherent account in which the events are connected by plausible causal links. All three conditions are met. Yet every source she employs was produced by the literate landowning class, and the voices of the peasants are entirely absent. The narrative is credible by the proposed definition, yet it tells the story exclusively from the perspective of those against whom the uprising was directed. What the background cases reveal is that credibility depends not only upon proximity, critical rigour, and coherence, but upon the representativeness of the sources with respect to all significant parties. A narrative built from one-sided sources, however rigorously evaluated, remains profoundly incomplete.

background_cases: When historians designated the fall of the Western Roman Empire in 476 as the boundary between antiquity and the medieval period, the periodisation was grounded in a political event of undeniable magnitude: the collapse of a centralised imperial authority that had governed the Mediterranean world for centuries.

When the French Revolution of 1789 was taken as the boundary between the early modern and modern periods, the periodisation was anchored in a political and social upheaval that transformed the structure of European governance and the vocabulary of political thought.

When historians designated the invention of the printing press around 1450 as a turning point, the periodisation was grounded in a technological innovation that transformed the production and dissemination of written material across an entire continent.
definition: A historical periodisation is well-grounded if and only if (i) each boundary between periods corresponds to an event or development that produced a rapid and fundamental transformation, (ii) the transformation is attested by diverse and independent sources, and (iii) the transformation affected the majority of the population within the relevant geographical region.
conditions: Each boundary corresponds to an event producing rapid and fundamental transformation
The transformation is attested by diverse and independent sources
The transformation affected the majority of the population within the relevant region
counterexample: Suppose a historian proposes a periodisation of the history of a large agrarian society in which a boundary is placed at the date of a palace coup that replaced one royal dynasty with another. The coup produced a rapid transformation of the ruling elite, is attested by diverse court chronicles, diplomatic correspondence, and foreign accounts, and affected the political life of the entire realm. All three conditions appear to be satisfied. Yet for the majority of the peasant population, whose daily lives were governed by the rhythm of the agricultural seasons, the change of dynasty produced no perceptible alteration. The transformation was confined to the political surface and did not penetrate the social, economic, or cultural life of the majority.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share — and what the definition fails to capture — is that the transformations that justify periodisation must affect not merely the political surface but the deep structures of social, economic, and cultural life. The fall of Rome, the French Revolution, and the printing press each transformed the conditions of daily existence for large populations. A definition that requires only rapid transformation, attestation, and majority impact without specifying the depth of the impact permits political events of limited social consequence to serve as period boundaries.
passage:
The most familiar periodisations of history are grounded in events whose transformative power extended well beyond the political sphere. The fall of the Western Roman Empire in 476 marked not only the end of a political order but the dissolution of an entire pattern of urban, economic, and cultural life across the western Mediterranean. The French Revolution transformed not merely the governance of France but the vocabulary and structures of political thought across Europe. The invention of printing around 1450 altered the conditions under which written material was produced, disseminated, and consumed.

These cases invite a general characterisation: a periodisation is well-grounded when each boundary corresponds to a rapid and fundamental transformation, attested by diverse sources, that affected the majority of the population. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from the history of agrarian societies. Suppose a historian places a period boundary at the date of a palace coup that replaced one royal dynasty with another. The coup produced a rapid transformation of the ruling elite, is attested by court chronicles, diplomatic letters, and foreign accounts, and affected the political life of the entire realm. All three conditions appear to be met. Yet for the majority of the peasant population, governed by the rhythm of the agricultural seasons, the change of dynasty produced no perceptible alteration in daily existence. What the background cases reveal is that the transformations justifying periodisation must penetrate the deep structures of social, economic, and cultural life, not merely the political surface. A definition that does not require depth of impact permits events of limited social consequence to serve as period boundaries.

definition: A historical narrative is explanatory if and only if it arranges events in a temporal sequence that reveals how earlier events brought about later ones, such that each event in the sequence is causally connected to its successor and the whole sequence renders the final outcome intelligible.
conditions: Events are arranged in a temporal sequence
Each event is causally connected to its successor
The whole sequence renders the final outcome intelligible
background_cases: The narrative of the outbreak of the First World War traces a sequence from the assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand through the activation of alliance systems to the declarations of war, where each step is causally connected to the next and the final outcome is rendered intelligible.

The narrative of the American Revolution arranges events from the Stamp Act through escalating colonial resistance to the Declaration of Independence, presenting a causal chain that makes the revolution intelligible.

The narrative of the fall of the Berlin Wall traces events from Gorbachev's reforms through popular protests to the opening of the border crossings, rendering the collapse of the wall causally intelligible.
counterexample: Consider a case in which a historian constructs a narrative of the collapse of the Western Roman Empire. She traces a temporal sequence from the crisis of the third century through barbarian incursions to the deposition of Romulus Augustulus in 476. Each event in her sequence is causally connected to its successor, and the whole sequence renders the final outcome intelligible. Yet an equally competent historian constructs an alternative narrative beginning with the economic transformations of the late Republic, proceeding through the Christianisation of the empire, and arriving at the same endpoint through an entirely different causal chain. Both narratives satisfy all three conditions, yet they offer incompatible explanations of the same outcome. The analysis provides no means of adjudicating between them, revealing that causal sequencing and intelligibility can be achieved by multiple, mutually exclusive narratives.
abductive_insight: The background cases suggest that a temporal causal sequence rendering an outcome intelligible constitutes an adequate historical explanation. Yet the case of Rome's fall reveals that the same outcome can be rendered intelligible by incompatible causal narratives, each satisfying the stated conditions equally well. The inductive generalisation from cases where a single dominant narrative exists conceals the underdetermination of historical explanation by narrative structure.
passage:
It is commonly held that a historical narrative explains an event when it arranges the antecedent events in a temporal sequence that exhibits the causal connections leading to the outcome, thereby rendering it intelligible. One may observe that this account is supported by several paradigmatic cases. The standard narrative of the outbreak of the Great War traces a chain from the assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand through the mobilisation of alliance systems to the declarations of war, presenting each link as causally connected to the next and making the conflagration intelligible. Similarly, narratives of the American Revolution and the fall of the Berlin Wall arrange events in sequences where each step causally explains its successor and the whole renders the final event comprehensible. From these cases, one might generalise that a temporal causal sequence yielding intelligibility is sufficient for historical explanation.

Yet we should hesitate to accept this generalisation without noting a serious difficulty. Let us consider the collapse of the Western Roman Empire. One historian constructs a narrative tracing a causal chain from the military crises of the third century through successive barbarian incursions to the deposition of the last Western emperor in 476. Each event is causally linked to the next, and the sequence renders the final collapse intelligible. Another historian, equally accomplished, constructs a very different narrative. She begins with the economic transformations of the late Republic, proceeds through the effects of the Christianisation of the empire upon civic virtue and military culture, and arrives at the same endpoint through an entirely different set of causal connections. Her narrative, too, satisfies all three conditions: it is temporally ordered, causally connected, and renders the outcome intelligible. These narratives are not complementary; they offer incompatible accounts of what brought the empire down. The analysis as stated provides no criterion for choosing between them, revealing that causal intelligibility can be achieved by multiple, mutually exclusive narratives of the same event. We are thus led to recognise that inductive generalisation from cases where a single narrative appears dominant conceals the fundamental underdetermination of historical explanation.

definition: A society has achieved moral progress if and only if it has expanded the scope of moral concern to include groups previously excluded, it has codified protections for these groups in law, and its members generally act in accordance with these expanded moral commitments.
conditions: It has expanded the scope of moral concern to include previously excluded groups
It has codified protections for these groups in law
Its members generally act in accordance with these expanded moral commitments
background_cases: The abolition of slavery in the British Empire, accompanied by legal protections for freed persons and a broad cultural shift against the institution, is widely regarded as an instance of moral progress.
The extension of suffrage to women in Western democracies, codified in law and accompanied by changing social attitudes, is taken as a clear case of moral progress.

The civil rights legislation of the 1960s in the United States, expanding legal protections for racial minorities and reflecting growing public commitment to equality, is held to exemplify moral progress.
counterexample: Suppose that a society expands its scope of moral concern to include animals, enacting comprehensive animal welfare legislation and seeing widespread public support for these laws. Its members generally act in accordance with these commitments, reducing cruelty and improving conditions for domesticated animals. Yet this same society simultaneously develops and deploys factory farming on an industrial scale, subjecting billions of animals to conditions that many of its own members would recognise as incompatible with the moral concern they profess. The society satisfies all three conditions with respect to a visible subset of animals while systematically violating its own expanded moral commitments on a vastly larger scale. The conditions are met at the level of legal codification and general attitude, yet the moral reality is one of profound inconsistency rather than genuine progress.
abductive_insight: The background cases suggest that expansion of moral concern, legal codification, and general compliance are sufficient for moral progress. Yet the animal welfare case reveals that a society may satisfy these conditions at the level of public commitment while systematically violating them in practice on a much larger scale. Inductive generalisation from cases where commitment and practice were broadly aligned conceals the possibility that formal moral progress may coexist with, or even enable, large-scale moral failure.
passage:
The concept of moral progress has been invoked to describe some of the most celebrated transformations in modern history. One may observe that the abolition of slavery in the British Empire involved an expansion of moral concern to include enslaved persons, was codified in law through the Emancipation Act of 1833, and was accompanied by a broad cultural shift against the institution. The extension of suffrage to women in Western democracies similarly expanded moral concern, was enshrined in legislation, and reflected genuine changes in social attitudes. The civil rights legislation of the 1960s in the United States followed the same pattern. From these cases, one might inductively conclude that a society achieves moral progress when it expands its scope of moral concern, codifies protections in law, and sees its members generally act in accordance with these commitments.

Yet we should hesitate to regard this generalisation as sufficient. Let us imagine a contemporary society that has expanded its moral concern to include non-human animals, enacting extensive animal welfare legislation prohibiting cruelty and mandating humane treatment. Public support for these measures is broad and sincere, and in their daily lives most citizens act in accordance with the new laws. The three conditions appear to be met. However, this same society has simultaneously developed industrial factory farming on an enormous scale, subjecting billions of animals to conditions of acute and prolonged suffering that plainly contradict the moral concern the society professes. The legal protections apply to pets and visible animals, while the concealed reality of industrial agriculture proceeds largely unchecked. The society satisfies the stated conditions at the level of law and public attitude, yet the moral reality involves a vast and systematic violation of the very commitments that are held to constitute progress. We are thus led to conclude that the inductive generalisation from cases where commitment and practice were broadly aligned obscures the possibility that formal moral progress may coexist with, or even serve to mask, large-scale moral failure in practice.

definition: A revolution succeeds in transforming society if and only if it overthrows the existing political order, establishes new governing institutions, and produces a lasting change in the distribution of social power.
conditions: It overthrows the existing political order
It establishes new governing institutions
It produces a lasting change in the distribution of social power
background_cases: The French Revolution of 1789 overthrew the monarchy, established republican institutions, and permanently altered the distribution of social power by abolishing feudal privileges.

The Russian Revolution of 1917 overthrew the Tsarist autocracy, established Soviet governing institutions, and produced a lasting redistribution of social power from the aristocracy and bourgeoisie to the proletarian state.

The Chinese Revolution of 1949 overthrew the Nationalist government, established the People's Republic, and fundamentally altered the distribution of social power through land reform and the elimination of the landlord class.
counterexample: Consider a case in which the Haitian Revolution of 1791-1804 overthrew the French colonial order, established a new republic with its own governing institutions, and produced a change in social power by ending slavery and placing formerly enslaved persons in positions of authority. All three conditions are met. Yet the international community imposed a crippling economic embargo, and France demanded an indemnity so large that it impoverished the new nation for over a century. The lasting change in the distribution of social power was largely confined to the domestic sphere and was undermined by external economic subjugation. The revolution satisfied the conditions in formal terms yet failed to achieve the genuine transformation that the analysis implies, because the analysis takes no account of the external structures of power that constrain revolutionary outcomes.
abductive_insight: The background cases suggest that overthrowing the old order, establishing new institutions, and redistributing social power are sufficient for revolutionary transformation. Yet the Haitian case reveals that these domestic achievements can be nullified by external forces, particularly economic coercion by powerful states. Inductive generalisation from revolutions that occurred in already powerful states overlooks the degree to which the success of revolution depends on the international context and the external structures of power that may undermine domestic transformation.
passage:
The question of what it means for a revolution to succeed in transforming society has been addressed by examining several major historical cases. The French Revolution of 1789 overthrew the monarchy, established new republican institutions, and permanently altered the distribution of social power by abolishing feudal privileges. The Russian Revolution of 1917 similarly overthrew an autocratic order, created new governing structures, and redistributed power. The Chinese Revolution of 1949 followed a comparable pattern, displacing the Nationalist government and fundamentally restructuring social relations through land reform. From these cases, one might inductively conclude that a revolution succeeds when it overthrows the existing order, establishes new institutions, and produces a lasting redistribution of social power.

Yet we should hesitate to suppose that these conditions are sufficient in all circumstances. Let us consider the Haitian Revolution of 1791 to 1804, in which the enslaved population of Saint-Domingue rose against their French colonial masters, defeated the armies of France, Spain, and Britain, and established the first independent Black republic in the Western hemisphere. New governing institutions were created, and formerly enslaved persons assumed positions of political authority. The distribution of social power was, within the borders of Haiti, radically altered. All three conditions are met. However, the international response was devastating. France demanded an enormous indemnity as the price of diplomatic recognition, an indemnity so large that its repayment consumed the nation's resources for more than a century. The great powers imposed economic isolation. The domestic redistribution of power, real as it was, was systematically undermined by external structures of economic and diplomatic coercion that the revolutionaries could not overcome. We are thus led to recognise that the inductive generalisation from revolutions in powerful states conceals a crucial variable: the international context within which revolutionary states must operate. A revolution that satisfies the stated conditions domestically may nonetheless fail to achieve genuine transformation when external powers act to nullify its achievements.
